# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define the term "chord rotation" in the slope-deflection method. How is it related to support settlement?**

**Model Answer:**
Chord rotation $\psi$ is the rigid-body rotation of the straight line connecting the two end supports of a beam member, caused by differential settlement of the supports. It is mathematically defined as:

$$
\psi = \frac{\Delta}{L}
$$

where $\Delta$ is the relative vertical settlement between the two ends and $L$ is the span of the member. Chord rotation is taken as **positive when clockwise**. It enters the slope-deflection equation as a kinematic term and directly contributes to the end moments even when no external loads are applied.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse chord rotation with end rotation. Remember: chord rotation is a **rigid-body** displacement of the entire member, while end rotations ($\theta_A$, $\theta_B$) are deformations relative to the chord.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: A propped cantilever beam of span L and flexural rigidity EI has its roller end settling by Δ. What is the fixed-end moment induced at the fixed end due to settlement alone?**

**Model Answer:**
For a propped cantilever with end A fixed and end B (roller) settled by $\Delta$, with $\theta_A = 0$ and $M_{BA} = 0$:

Using the slope-deflection equation:

$$
M_{BA} = \frac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_B - 3\psi) = 0
$$

Solving: $\theta_B = \dfrac{3\psi}{2} = \dfrac{3\Delta}{2L}$

Substituting back:

$$
M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2 \cdot 0 + \frac{3\Delta}{2L} - \frac{3\Delta}{L}\right) = \frac{2EI}{L} \cdot \left(-\frac{3\Delta}{2L}\right) = -\frac{3EI\Delta}{L^2}
$$

The magnitude of the fixed-end moment at A due to settlement is:

$$
\boxed{\vert M_{AB} \vert = \frac{3EI\Delta}{L^2}}
$$

The negative sign indicates it acts **anticlockwise** on the member end at A.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A continuous beam ABC** has the following properties:
- Span AB: Length = 4 m, EI = constant = 8000 kN·m²
- Span BC: Length = 4 m, EI = constant = 8000 kN·m²
- Support A is **fixed**, Support B is a roller, Support C is **fixed**
- A uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **20 kN/m** acts over the entire span AB only
- Support B settles by **10 mm** (downward)

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Using the Slope-Deflection Method, determine the end moments of span AB due to the UDL alone (ignore settlement in this part).

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Considering the support settlement of 10 mm at B, compute the **additional moments** induced in spans AB and BC due to settlement alone.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1: Identify Unknowns**

For span AB (A fixed, B roller):
- $\theta_A = 0$ (fixed)
- $\theta_B \neq 0$
- $\psi_{AB} = 0$ (ignore settlement)
- $FEM_{AB} = -\dfrac{wL^2}{12} = -\dfrac{20 \times 4^2}{12} = -\dfrac{320}{12} = -26.67$ kN·m
- $FEM_{BA} = +\dfrac{wL^2}{12} = +26.67$ kN·m

**Step 2: Write Slope-Deflection Equations for Span AB**

$$
M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{L}(2 \cdot 0 + \theta_B - 0) + FEM_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{L}\theta_B - 26.67
$$

Substituting $\dfrac{2EI}{L} = \dfrac{2 \times 8000}{4} = 4000$:

$$
M_{AB} = 4000 \theta_B - 26.67 \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

$$
M_{BA} = \frac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_B + 0 - 0) + FEM_{BA} = 4000 \times 2\theta_B + 26.67 = 8000 \theta_B + 26.67 \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Boundary Condition at B**

Since B is a roller (simply supported), $M_{BA} = 0$:

$$
8000 \theta_B + 26.67 = 0 \implies \theta_B = -\frac{26.67}{8000} = -0.003333 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $M_{AB}$**

$$
M_{AB} = 4000 \times (-0.003333) - 26.67 = -13.33 - 26.67 = -40.0 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Answer for Part (a):**

$$
\boxed{M_{AB} = -40 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{BA} = 0, \quad \theta_B = -0.003333 \text{ rad}}
$$

The negative sign on $M_{AB}$ indicates an **anticlockwise moment** at A on the member.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

Now consider **settlement alone** (no UDL on either span).

**Step 1: Compute Chord Rotations**

- $\psi_{AB} = \dfrac{\Delta_B - \Delta_A}{L_{AB}} = \dfrac{10 \text{ mm} - 0}{4000 \text{ mm}} = 0.0025$ rad (positive, clockwise chord rotation as B goes down relative to A)
- $\psi_{BC} = \dfrac{\Delta_C - \Delta_B}{L_{BC}} = \dfrac{0 - 10 \text{ mm}}{4000 \text{ mm}} = -0.0025$ rad (negative, since B is lower than C, the chord of BC rotates anticlockwise)

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle but Critical Point:** The chord rotation of span BC has the **opposite sign** to that of span AB because the relative settlement direction reverses. This is a frequent source of error in KTU scripts.

**Step 2: Write Slope-Deflection Equations (No External Loads, so FEM = 0)**

For span AB with $\theta_A = 0$:

$$
M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{L}(2 \cdot 0 + \theta_B - 3\psi_{AB}) = 4000(\theta_B - 3 \times 0.0025)
$$

$$
M_{AB} = 4000(\theta_B - 0.0075) \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

$$
M_{BA} = \frac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_B + 0 - 3\psi_{AB}) = 4000(2\theta_B - 0.0075) \quad \text{...(iv)}
$$

For span BC with $\theta_C = 0$:

$$
M_{BC} = \frac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_B + 0 - 3\psi_{BC}) = 4000(2\theta_B - 3 \times (-0.0025))
$$

$$
M_{BC} = 4000(2\theta_B + 0.0075) \quad \text{...(v)}
$$

$$
M_{CB} = \frac{2EI}{L}(2 \cdot 0 + \theta_B - 3\psi_{BC}) = 4000(\theta_B + 0.0075) \quad \text{...(vi)}
$$

**Step 3: Joint Equilibrium at B**

Sum of moments at joint B equals zero (no external moment):

$$
M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0
$$

Substituting from (iv) and (v):

$$
4000(2\theta_B - 0.0075) + 4000(2\theta_B + 0.0075) = 0
$$

$$
8000 \theta_B - 30 + 8000 \theta_B + 30 = 0
$$

$$
16000 \theta_B = 0 \implies \theta_B = 0
$$

**Step 4: Compute End Moments Due to Settlement**

With $\theta_B = 0$:

From (iii): $M_{AB} = 4000(0 - 0.0075) = -30$ kN·m

From (iv): $M_{BA} = 4000(0 - 0.0075) = -30$ kN·m

From (v): $M_{BC} = 4000(0 + 0.0075) = +30$ kN·m

From (vi): $M_{CB} = 4000(0 + 0.0075) = +30$ kN·m

**Answer for Part (b):**

$$
\boxed{M_{AB} = -30 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{BA} = -30 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{BC} = +30 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{CB} = +30 \text{ kN·m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Sign convention slips cost 2-3 marks.** Students often write chord rotation as positive for both spans, missing the reversal.
> 2. **Joint equilibrium sign errors are the #1 reason for full marks denial.** The sum at joint B is $M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$, not $M_{AB} + M_{BC} = 0$.
> 3. **Units consistency:** Always convert settlement $\Delta$ from **mm to m** before using in formulas with EI in kN·m².

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Alternative Choice

A two-span continuous beam **ABC** has both spans of length **5 m** and constant $EI = 10000$ kN·m².
- Support A: **Fixed**
- Support B: **Roller**
- Support C: **Fixed**
- A central point load of **50 kN** acts at midspan of BC
- Support C settles by **8 mm** (downward)

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Using the **Moment Distribution Method**, determine the end moments in span BC due to the point load alone.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Determine the **additional end moments** due to the settlement of support C.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — Moment Distribution Method

**Step 1: Compute Fixed-End Moments for Span BC (Point Load at Center)**

$$
FEM_{BC} = -\frac{PL}{8} = -\frac{50 \times 5}{8} = -31.25 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
FEM_{CB} = +\frac{PL}{8} = +31.25 \text{ kN·m}
$$

(FEM for span AB = 0 since no load)

**Step 2: Compute Stiffness and Distribution Factors at Joint B**

Stiffness of AB (far end A is fixed): $K_{BA} = \dfrac{I}{L} = \dfrac{I}{5}$

Stiffness of BC (far end C is fixed): $K_{BC} = \dfrac{I}{L} = \dfrac{I}{5}$

Total stiffness at B: $\sum K = \dfrac{2I}{5}$

Distribution factors at B:

$$
DF_{BA} = \frac{I/5}{2I/5} = 0.5
$$

$$
DF_{BC} = \frac{I/5}{2I/5} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3: Moment Distribution Table**

| Joint | A | B | B | C |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Member | AB | BA | BC | CB |
| Distribution Factor | — | 0.5 | 0.5 | — |
| Initial FEM (kN·m) | 0 | 0 | $-31.25$ | $+31.25$ |
| Joint B balance | 0 | $+15.625$ | $+15.625$ | 0 |
| Carry-over to A and C | $+7.8125$ | 0 | 0 | $+7.8125$ |
| **Final Moments (kN·m)** | **$+7.8125$** | **$+15.625$** | **$-15.625$** | **$+39.0625$** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Carry-over rule:** Carry-over factor from near end to far end is +1/2. The carried-over moment from B to A is $\dfrac{+15.625}{2} = +7.8125$ kN·m.

**Answer for Part (a):**

$$
\boxed{M_{BC} = -15.625 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{CB} = +39.0625 \text{ kN·m}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Settlement of Support C

**Step 1: Settlement-Induced FEMs**

For span BC, support C settles by $\Delta = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m relative to B (which does not settle).

Chord rotation: $\psi_{BC} = \dfrac{\Delta_C - \Delta_B}{L_{BC}} = \dfrac{0.008 - 0}{5} = 0.0016$ rad (clockwise chord rotation, positive)

Settlement FEMs (load FEMs = 0):

$$
FEM_{BC}^{\text{sett}} = -\frac{6EI\Delta}{L^2} = -\frac{6 \times 10000 \times 0.008}{5^2} = -\frac{480}{25} = -19.2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
FEM_{CB}^{\text{sett}} = +\frac{6EI\Delta}{L^2} = +19.2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2: Moment Distribution for Settlement**

The same DFs apply. Distribution proceeds identically:

| Joint | A | B | B | C |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Distribution Factor | — | 0.5 | 0.5 | — |
| Initial FEM (kN·m) | 0 | 0 | $-19.2$ | $+19.2$ |
| Joint B balance | 0 | $+9.6$ | $+9.6$ | 0 |
| Carry-over | $+4.8$ | 0 | 0 | $+4.8$ |
| **Final Moments (kN·m)** | **$+4.8$** | **$+9.6$** | **$-9.6$** | **$+24.0$** |

**Answer for Part (b):**

$$
\boxed{M_{AB}^{\text{sett}} = +4.8 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{BA}^{\text{sett}} = +9.6 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{BC}^{\text{sett}} = -9.6 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{CB}^{\text{sett}} = +24.0 \text{ kN·m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forget to update DFs** — some students use $K_{modified} = 0.75 I/L$ for spans with far-end pinned. If the far end is fixed, use $K = I/L$ (do not reduce). In this problem, both A and C are fixed, so no modification.
> 2. **Forget to add load FEMs and settlement FEMs together** when both exist simultaneously. The total FEM is the algebraic sum.
> 3. **Stopping iteration too early** — converge when moments change by less than 0.01 kN·m per cycle. Two cycles are usually sufficient for single-joint problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Support classification**:
  - *Fixed*: prevents translation and rotation → develops $H$, $V$, $M$.
  - *Pinned/Roller*: prevents translation only → develops $H$, $V$ or $V$ only.
  - *Free*: no restraints.

- **Degree of Static Indeterminacy (DoI)**:
  - Propped cantilever: **DoI = 1**.
  - Fixed-fixed beam: **DoI = 3** (in general, 2 rotational + 1 sway).
  - Continuous beam: Sum over intermediate supports of (number of restraints at support − 1).

- **Slope-deflection equation (master form)**:
  $$
  M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi) + FEM_{AB}
  $$

- **Chord rotation definition**:
  $$
  \psi = \frac{\Delta_B - \Delta_A}{L}
  $$
  Positive when clockwise (B is below A).

- **Settlement-induced FEMs**:
  $$
  FEM^{\text{sett}}_{AB} = -\frac{6EI\Delta}{L^2}, \quad FEM^{\text{sett}}_{BA} = +\frac{6EI\Delta}{L^2}
  $$

- **Standard FEMs to memorize**:
  - UDL $w$ over full span: $\pm wL^2/12$.
  - Point load $P$ at center: $\pm PL/8$.
  - Point load $P$ at distance $a$ from A: $-Pab^2/L^2$ at A, $+Pa^2b/L^2$ at B.

- **Moment distribution essentials**:
  - $DF_{ij} = K_{ij}/\sum K$ at joint $i$.
  - Modified stiffness for far-end pinned: $K_{mod} = 0.75 I/L$.
  - Carry-over factor = $+1/2$.

- **Sign convention (KTU standard)**:
  - Clockwise end moments on members are positive.
  - Clockwise rotations are positive.
  - Clockwise chord rotation is positive.

- **Engineering insight on settlement**:
  - Even small settlements (10 mm) produce significant moments in stiff beams (EI large).
  - Magnitude scales with $EI/L^2$ — stiffer and shorter beams experience larger settlement moments.
  - Always check settlement-induced moments separately and add (superpose) with load-induced moments.

- **Solver's quick test**: After solving any problem, verify **overall equilibrium** ($\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$ for the whole beam). If violated, a sign error exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark Part B question, the marks are typically distributed as:
> - 2 marks for correctly identifying the kinematic DOFs.
> - 3 marks for correctly computing FEMs (including settlement FEMs if applicable).
> - 4 marks for setting up and solving the slope-deflection equations / moment distribution table.
> - 3 marks for computing reactions and drawing BMD/SFD.
> - 2 marks for units, sign convention, and overall equilibrium verification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beams and Rigid Jointed Frames (with and without sway)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST403 - ANALYSIS OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Beams and Rigid Jointed Frames (with and without sway)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Stiffness Method (Displacement Method / Equilibrium Method)** of structural analysis, the primary unknowns are the **joint displacements** of a structure. For beams and rigid-jointed plane frames, these displacements consist of:

1. **Rotational Degree of Freedom (DOF):** Independent joint rotations $\theta_i$ at rigid joints.
2. **Translational Degree of Freedom / Sway DOF:** Independent joint translations $\Delta_i$ (sway) of rigid-jointed frames whose members undergo **chord rotation** $\psi = \Delta/L$ (i.e., columns in a frame undergoing side-sway).

A structure is said to be a **"Frame without sway"** (or **braced frame / non-sway frame**) when joint translations are either physically prevented (by supports/struts) or geometrically constrained to zero (e.g., symmetric portal frames with symmetric vertical loads). A **"Frame with sway"** is one in which at least one independent lateral translation exists and must be treated as an additional unknown.

The stiffness method proceeds by:
- **Step 1:** Identifying kinematic indeterminacy (number of unknown rotations + unknown sway DOFs).
- **Step 2:** Writing the global stiffness equation $\mathbf{K}\boldsymbol{\Delta} = \mathbf{P} - \mathbf{P_0}$ where $\mathbf{P_0}$ is the fixed-joint force vector.
- **Step 3:** Solving for $\boldsymbol{\Delta}$.
- **Step 4:** Recovering member-end actions via slope-deflection equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** *Kinematic Indeterminacy (DKI)* = Number of independent joint rotations + Number of independent joint translations (sway DOFs). It is the number of unknown displacements in the stiffness method.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a wooden frame made of sticks joined at corners with tight clamps. If you push the top of the frame sideways, every joint rotates a little AND the whole frame tilts (translates). The stiffness method treats the structure like a **spring system**: each joint has a "rotational spring stiffness" and possibly a "translational spring stiffness."

- **Beams** are like a 1D row of such springs — only rotations occur (no sway, as supports usually prevent translation).
- **Frames without sway** are like a 2D frame whose top is held horizontally by a stiff wall — joints rotate but don't translate sideways.
- **Frames with sway** are like a 2D frame free to move sideways — joints rotate AND the top moves sideways by an unknown amount $\Delta$.

> [!NOTE]
> The "stiffness coefficient" $k_{ij}$ is the **force (or moment) at coordinate $i$ required to produce a unit displacement (or rotation) at coordinate $j$ while keeping all other coordinates fixed.** Think of it as the "resistance" of that degree of freedom.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $EI$ | Flexural rigidity of member | kN·m² (or N·mm²) |
| $L$ | Member length | m |
| $k = 4EI/L$ | Rotational stiffness of a far-end fixed member | kN·m/rad |
| $\theta$ | Joint rotation | radians (rad) |
| $\Delta$ | Joint translation / sway | m |
| $\psi = \Delta/L$ | Chord rotation of a member | rad |
| $M$ | Bending moment | kN·m |
| $V$ | Shear force | kN |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — How the Stiffness Method Works

### A. The Slope-Deflection Master Equation

The fundamental relationship between member-end moments and end rotations (including chord rotation) is:

$$M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_A + \theta_B - \frac{3\Delta}{L}\right) + FEM_{AB}$$

$$M_{BA} = \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_B + \theta_A - \frac{3\Delta}{L}\right) + FEM_{BA}$$

**Logic behind each term:**
- $2\theta_A$: The end where the moment is being calculated gets a weight of **2** (carry-over factor of 1/2 doubled).
- $\theta_B$: The far end contributes with weight **1**.
- $-3\Delta/L = -3\psi$: The chord rotation (sway) reduces the moment magnitude if sway is in the direction that relieves rotation.
- $FEM$: Fixed-End Moments from external loads on the member (precomputed, known quantities).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Clockwise moments and clockwise rotations on the member end are taken as **POSITIVE** for both $M$ and $\theta$ (the standard slope-deflection sign convention).

### B. The Member Stiffness Matrix (Slope-Deflection Form)

For a single prismatic beam member (with no relative transverse displacement, $\Delta = 0$), the member stiffness equation in matrix form is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} M_{AB} \\ M_{BA} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2EI}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \theta_A \\ \theta_B \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} FEM_{AB} \\ FEM_{BA} \end{bmatrix}$$

For a member **with** chord rotation $\psi$ (a column in a sway frame):

$$\begin{bmatrix} M_{AB} \\ M_{BA} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2EI}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \theta_A \\ \theta_B \end{bmatrix} - \frac{6EI}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\psi - \begin{bmatrix} FEM_{AB} \\ FEM_{BA} \end{bmatrix}$$

### C. Procedure for Continuous Beams (No Sway)

1. **Compute** Fixed-End Moments (FEMs) for each loaded span from standard tables.
2. **Identify** all rigid-joint rotations $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$ (kinematic indeterminacy = number of interior continuous supports).
3. **Write** the slope-deflection equation for **every member** in terms of the unknown rotations.
4. **Apply** joint equilibrium: $\sum M_{\text{joint}} = 0$ at every interior joint.
5. **Solve** the resulting set of linear equations for $\theta_i$.
6. **Substitute** back to obtain all member-end moments and draw the BMD/SFD.

### D. Procedure for Rigid Frames Without Sway

Identical to continuous beams, but you have more members meeting at each joint. The joint equilibrium equation becomes:

$$\sum M_{\text{at joint } i} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{\text{all members at } i} M_{ij} = 0$$

The global stiffness coefficient $K_{ii}$ at a rigid joint is simply the sum of the rotational stiffness contributions of all members meeting at that joint:

$$K_{ii} = \sum_{j \in \text{members at } i} \frac{4EI_{ij}}{L_{ij}}$$

### E. Procedure for Rigid Frames With Sway

1. **Identify** rotational DOFs $\theta_i$ and sway DOF $\Delta$.
2. **Write** slope-deflection equations including the $-3\psi = -3\Delta/L$ term for each column.
3. **Apply** joint moment equilibrium: $\sum M = 0$ at every rigid joint.
4. **Apply** joint shear equilibrium: $\sum H = 0$ (one independent shear equation per sway DOF).
   - For a column $AB$ (bottom fixed, top free to translate by $\Delta$):
   
$$V_{AB} = \frac{6EI}{L^2}(\theta_A + \theta_B) - \frac{12EI}{L^3}\Delta + V_{AB}^{FEM}$$

5. **Solve** the augmented system of equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why shear equilibrium?** When the frame sways, columns must resist a net horizontal force. The internal shear in the columns must balance the applied external horizontal loads.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi) + FEM_{AB}$ | Slope-deflection: member with chord rotation |
| 2 | $M_{AB} = \frac{4EI}{L}\theta_A + \frac{2EI}{L}\theta_B + FEM_{AB}$ | Slope-deflection: beam, no chord rotation |
| 3 | $\psi = \Delta/L$ | Chord rotation of a column undergoing sway $\Delta$ |
| 4 | $K_{ii} = \sum 4EI/L$ | Diagonal stiffness coefficient at a rigid joint |
| 5 | $K_{ij} = 2EI/L$ | Off-diagonal stiffness coefficient (joints $i,j$ connected) |
| 6 | $V_{AB} = \frac{6EI}{L^2}(\theta_A + \theta_B) - \frac{12EI}{L^3}\Delta + V_{AB}^{FEM}$ | Shear in a column with both ends free to rotate and translate |
| 7 | $V_{AB}^F = \frac{P \cdot a \cdot b^2}{L^3} + \frac{P \cdot a^2 \cdot b}{L^3}$ (point load) | Fixed-end reaction (general) — use as per standard tables |
| 8 | $FEM = \frac{wL^2}{12}$ (UDL) | Fixed-end moment for uniformly distributed load $w$ |
| 9 | $FEM = \frac{PL}{8}$ (central point load) | Fixed-end moment for central point load $P$ |
| 10 | $\mathbf{K}\boldsymbol{\Delta} = \mathbf{P} - \mathbf{P_0}$ | Global stiffness equation (matrix form) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Pitfall:** When writing slope-deflection equations, ALWAYS keep the sign convention consistent. The $-3\Delta/L$ term is **negative** because positive $\Delta$ (sway in chosen positive direction) causes chord rotation that **opposes** the positive end rotations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The stiffness method is the **backbone of modern structural analysis software** (STAAD.Pro, ETABS, SAP2000, ANSYS Mechanical). Every finite element analysis (FEA) of a building, bridge, aircraft, or offshore platform uses the matrix stiffness formulation. Beams and rigid-jointed frames are the entry-level problems that train engineers to:
- Recognize indeterminacy and choose displacement unknowns.
- Set up global stiffness matrices for real structures.
- Apply boundary conditions to obtain solutions.

In **seismic design**, sway analysis of frames is critical — the lateral stiffness $\sum 12EI/L^3$ of columns determines how a building drifts under earthquake loads.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Slope-Deflection Equation from First Principles

### Setup

Consider a prismatic beam $AB$ of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$. The near end $A$ rotates by $\theta_A$, the far end $B$ rotates by $\theta_B$, and the member undergoes a **chord rotation** $\psi$ (the chord $AB$ rotates by angle $\psi$ relative to its original position). External loads on the member produce fixed-end moments $FEM_{AB}$ and $FEM_{BA}$.

### Step 1 — Total End Rotations Relative to the Chord

The end rotation **relative to the chord** is:

$$\phi_A = \theta_A - \psi \quad \text{(at end } A\text{)}$$

$$\phi_B = \theta_B - \psi \quad \text{(at end } B\text{)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Standard Fixed-Fixed Beam Stiffness Relation

For a beam whose ends rotate by $\phi_A$ and $\phi_B$ **relative to the chord** (with no loads on the member), the end moments are:

$$M_{AB}^{\text{(no load)}} = \frac{4EI}{L}\phi_A + \frac{2EI}{L}\phi_B$$

$$M_{BA}^{\text{(no load)}} = \frac{4EI}{L}\phi_B + \frac{2EI}{L}\phi_A$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $\phi_A = \theta_A - \psi$ and $\phi_B = \theta_B - \psi$

$$M_{AB}^{\text{(no load)}} = \frac{4EI}{L}(\theta_A - \psi) + \frac{2EI}{L}(\theta_B - \psi)$$

$$= \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi\right)$$

Similarly:

$$M_{BA}^{\text{(no load)}} = \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_B + \theta_A - 3\psi\right)$$

### Step 4 — Add the Fixed-End Moments (Superposition)

When the same member is loaded externally, the **principle of superposition** gives the total end moments:

$$\boxed{M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi\right) + FEM_{AB}}$$

$$\boxed{M_{BA} = \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_B + \theta_A - 3\psi\right) + FEM_{BA}}$$

This is the **slope-deflection equation** in its complete form.

## 3.2 Derivation: Shear in a Swaying Column

### Setup

A column $AB$ of length $L$, fixed at base $A$, free (but tied to a rigid slab) at top $B$. The top translates by $\Delta$ horizontally. Rotations: $\theta_A$ and $\theta_B$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Column Shear from End Moments

Taking moments about the base of the column (sum of moments of forces above the base about the base equals zero):

$$V_{AB} \cdot L = M_{AB} + M_{BA} + (\text{shear-induced moment, if any lateral load exists on the column})$$

For a column with **no transverse load** between the ends:

$$V_{AB} = -\frac{M_{AB} + M_{BA}}{L}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Slope-Deflection Equations

$$V_{AB} = -\frac{1}{L}\left[\frac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi) + FEM_{AB} + \frac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_B + \theta_A - 3\psi) + FEM_{BA}\right]$$

$$= -\frac{1}{L}\left[\frac{2EI}{L}\left(3\theta_A + 3\theta_B - 6\psi\right) + (FEM_{AB} + FEM_{BA})\right]$$

$$= -\frac{6EI}{L^2}(\theta_A + \theta_B - 2\psi) - \frac{(FEM_{AB} + FEM_{BA})}{L}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $\psi = \Delta/L$

$$V_{AB} = -\frac{6EI}{L^2}(\theta_A + \theta_B) + \frac{12EI}{L^3}\Delta - \frac{(FEM_{AB} + FEM_{BA})}{L}$$

With the sign convention that **shear in the direction of positive $\Delta$ is taken as positive**, we get:

$$\boxed{V_{AB} = \frac{6EI}{L^2}(\theta_A + \theta_B) - \frac{12EI}{L^3}\Delta + V_{AB}^{FEM}}$$

where $V_{AB}^{FEM} = -\frac{(FEM_{AB} + FEM_{BA})}{L}$ is the fixed-end shear in the column.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Continuous Beam (No Sway) — KTU Standard Type

**Problem:** A continuous beam $ABC$ has spans $AB = 4$ m and $BC = 4$ m. Support $A$ is fixed, $B$ is a roller, $C$ is a roller. Uniformly distributed load $w = 30$ kN/m acts over both spans. $EI$ is constant. Compute all support moments and reactions using the stiffness method.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Fixed-End Moments

For UDL on a fixed-fixed span: $FEM = wL^2/12$ (with sign: left end = $-wL^2/12$, right end = $+wL^2/12$).

For span $AB$: $FEM_{AB} = -\frac{30 \times 4^2}{12} = -40$ kN·m, $FEM_{BA} = +40$ kN·m.
For span $BC$: $FEM_{BC} = -40$ kN·m, $FEM_{CB} = +40$ kN·m.

### Step 2 — Kinematic Indeterminacy

Joints $A$ and $C$ are fixed/simple ends, so $\theta_A = \theta_C = 0$. The only unknown is $\theta_B$. **DKI = 1.**

### Step 3 — Slope-Deflection Equations (with $\psi = 0$ for beams)

$$M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{4}(2 \cdot 0 + \theta_B) + (-40) = \frac{EI}{2}\theta_B - 40$$

$$M_{BA} = \frac{2EI}{4}(2\theta_B + 0) + 40 = EI\,\theta_B + 40$$

$$M_{BC} = \frac{2EI}{4}(2\theta_B + 0) + (-40) = EI\,\theta_B - 40$$

$$M_{CB} = \frac{2EI}{4}(2 \cdot 0 + \theta_B) + 40 = \frac{EI}{2}\theta_B + 40$$

### Step 4 — Joint Equilibrium at $B$

$$\sum M_B = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$$

$$(EI\,\theta_B + 40) + (EI\,\theta_B - 40) = 0$$

$$2EI\,\theta_B = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_B = 0$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Back to Find End Moments

$$M_{AB} = -40 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{BA} = +40 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{BC} = -40 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{CB} = +40 \text{ kN·m}$$

### Step 6 — Reactions (by statics of each span)

For span $AB$ (load 30 kN/m over 4 m, end moments $M_A = -40$, $M_B = +40$):
- Vertical reaction at $A$: $V_A = \frac{wL}{2} - \frac{M_B - M_A}{L} = 60 - \frac{40-(-40)}{4} = 60 - 20 = 40$ kN.
- Vertical reaction at $B$ (from span $AB$ side): $V_B^{(AB)} = 60 + 20 = 80$ kN.

By symmetry, $V_C = 40$ kN, $V_B^{(BC)} = 80$ kN. Total $V_B = 80 + 80 = 160$ kN.

**Result:** Symmetry yields $\theta_B = 0$ as expected. The beam behaves like two back-to-back fixed-fixed beams. (This is the expected KTU answer for symmetric loading.)

## 3.4 Worked Example: Portal Frame With Sway — KTU Standard Type

**Problem:** A portal frame $ABCD$ has columns $AB$ and $CD$ of length 4 m each, beam $BC$ of length 6 m. All members have constant $EI$. A horizontal load $H = 20$ kN acts at the beam level at $B$ (joint $B$ moves by $\Delta$ to the right). Supports $A$ and $D$ are fixed. Find all member-end moments.

### Step 1 — Kinematic Indeterminacy

$\theta_B$, $\theta_C$ (rotational), and $\Delta$ (sway). **DKI = 3**.

### Step 2 — Slope-Deflection Equations

Chord rotation for both columns: $\psi = \Delta/4$. For the beam $BC$, chord rotation $= 0$ (it translates but does not rotate; the beam ends undergo the same vertical displacement, so $\Delta$ doesn't induce chord rotation in a horizontal beam).

**Column $AB$:**
$$M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{4}\left(2\cdot 0 + \theta_B - \frac{3\Delta}{4}\right) + 0 = \frac{EI}{2}\theta_B - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta$$

$$M_{BA} = \frac{2EI}{4}\left(2\theta_B + 0 - \frac{3\Delta}{4}\right) + 0 = EI\,\theta_B - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta$$

**Column $CD$** (by symmetry, taking $\theta_C$ as the rotation):
$$M_{DC} = \frac{2EI}{4}\left(2\cdot 0 + \theta_C - \frac{3\Delta}{4}\right) + 0 = \frac{EI}{2}\theta_C - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta$$

$$M_{CD} = \frac{2EI}{4}\left(2\theta_C + 0 - \frac{3\Delta}{4}\right) + 0 = EI\,\theta_C - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta$$

**Beam $BC$:**
$$M_{BC} = \frac{2EI}{6}(2\theta_B + \theta_C) + 0 = \frac{2EI}{3}\theta_B + \frac{EI}{3}\theta_C$$

$$M_{CB} = \frac{2EI}{6}(2\theta_C + \theta_B) + 0 = \frac{2EI}{3}\theta_C + \frac{EI}{3}\theta_B$$

### Step 3 — Joint Equilibrium

**At $B$:** $M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$

$$EI\,\theta_B - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta + \frac{2EI}{3}\theta_B + \frac{EI}{3}\theta_C = 0$$

Dividing by $EI$:

$$\frac{5}{3}\theta_B + \frac{1}{3}\theta_C - \frac{3}{8}\Delta = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**At $C$:** $M_{CB} + M_{CD} = 0$

$$\frac{EI}{3}\theta_B + \frac{2EI}{3}\theta_C + EI\,\theta_C - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{3}\theta_B + \frac{5}{3}\theta_C - \frac{3}{8}\Delta = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 4 — Shear Equilibrium (Sway Equation)

By symmetry, the sway is symmetric: $\theta_B = -\theta_C$ and the horizontal force is resisted equally by both columns. Take sum of horizontal forces:

$$H = V_{AB} + V_{DC}$$

where $V_{AB} = -\frac{M_{AB} + M_{BA}}{L}$ and similarly for $V_{DC}$:

$$V_{AB} = -\frac{(M_{AB} + M_{BA})}{4} = -\frac{1}{4}\left[\left(\frac{EI}{2}\theta_B - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta\right) + \left(EI\,\theta_B - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta\right)\right]$$

$$= -\frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{3EI}{2}\theta_B - \frac{3EI}{4}\Delta\right] = -\frac{3EI}{8}\theta_B + \frac{3EI}{16}\Delta$$

By symmetry, $V_{DC} = -\frac{3EI}{8}\theta_C + \frac{3EI}{16}\Delta$.

$$H = V_{AB} + V_{DC} = -\frac{3EI}{8}(\theta_B + \theta_C) + \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta = 20$$

Dividing by $EI$ (assuming $EI = 1$ for simplicity, restore later):

$$-\frac{3}{8}(\theta_B + \theta_C) + \frac{3}{8}\Delta = \frac{20}{EI} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

### Step 5 — Solve the System

From symmetry, $\theta_C = -\theta_B$ (assuming the loading and frame are symmetric in the anti-symmetric sway mode; in fact, with symmetric geometry and anti-symmetric load, $\theta_B = -\theta_C$).

Substitute $\theta_C = -\theta_B$ into (i):

$$\frac{5}{3}\theta_B - \frac{1}{3}\theta_B - \frac{3}{8}\Delta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{4}{3}\theta_B = \frac{3}{8}\Delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_B = \frac{9}{32}\Delta$$

Equation (ii) gives the same result (since $\theta_C = -\theta_B$).

Substitute $\theta_B + \theta_C = 0$ into (iii):

$$\frac{3}{8}\Delta = \frac{20}{EI} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta = \frac{160}{3EI}$$

Then:

$$\theta_B = \frac{9}{32} \cdot \frac{160}{3EI} = \frac{15}{EI}, \quad \theta_C = -\frac{15}{EI}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Member-End Moments

**Column $AB$ (bottom to top):**
$$M_{AB} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{15}{EI}\cdot EI - \frac{3}{8}\cdot\frac{160}{3EI}\cdot EI = 7.5 - 20 = -12.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{BA} = \frac{15}{EI}\cdot EI - \frac{3}{8}\cdot\frac{160}{3EI}\cdot EI = 15 - 20 = -5.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Column $CD$ (by anti-symmetry):**
$$M_{DC} = -12.5 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{CD} = -5.0 \text{ kN·m}$$
(signs are anti-symmetric: $M_{CD} = +5.0$ kN·m if the anti-symmetric rotation reverses.)

**Beam $BC$:**
$$M_{BC} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot 15 + \frac{1}{3}\cdot(-15) = 10 - 5 = 5.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{CB} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot(-15) + \frac{1}{3}\cdot 15 = -10 + 5 = -5.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Result:** All end moments are obtained, and the BMD can be drawn.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stiffness Method Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **complete operational architecture** of the stiffness method as applied to beams and rigid-jointed frames (with and without sway).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: Define Structure Geometry and Loads] --> B[STEP 2: Identify Kinematic Indeterminacy]
    B --> C{Step 3: Sway Exists?}
    C -- NO --> D[DKI = Joint Rotations Only]
    C -- YES --> E[DKI = Joint Rotations + Sway DOF]
    D --> F[STEP 4: Compute Fixed End Moments FEMs from Standard Tables]
    E --> F
    F --> G[STEP 5: Write Slope Deflection Equations for Each Member]
    G --> H[STEP 6: Apply Joint Moment Equilibrium: Sum M Joint = 0]
    H --> I{Sway?}
    I -- NO --> K[STEP 8: Solve Linear System for Theta i]
    I -- YES --> J[STEP 7: Apply Shear Equilibrium: Sum H = 0]
    J --> K
    K --> L[STEP 9: Substitute Theta i and Delta into Slope Deflection Equations]
    L --> M[STEP 10: Compute Member End Moments M AB and M BA]
    M --> N[STEP 11: Draw Bending Moment Diagram BMD and SFD]
    N --> O[STEP 12: Verify with Joint Equilibrium and Shear Check]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Continuous Beam DOF Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Fixed Support A: theta A = 0] -->|Span AB length L1| B[Rigid Joint B: theta B = unknown]
    B -->|Span BC length L2| C[Rigid Joint C: theta C = unknown]
    C -->|Span CD length L3| D[Roller Support D: theta D = 0]
```

**Interpretation:** For an $N$-span continuous beam with all spans loaded, the number of unknown rotations equals the number of interior rigid supports. Each unknown $\theta$ corresponds to one equilibrium equation $\sum M_{\text{joint}} = 0$.

## 4.3 Schematic: Portal Frame With Sway — DOF Identification

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fixed Base A: theta A = 0] -->|Column AB| B[Joint B: theta B = unknown and Delta = unknown]
    B -->|Beam BC length Lb| C[Joint C: theta C = unknown and Delta = unknown]
    C -->|Column CD| D[Fixed Base D: theta D = 0]
```

**Interpretation:** Joints $B$ and $C$ each have a rotational DOF. Both joints move horizontally by the **same** amount $\Delta$ (assuming a rigid horizontal beam — this is the standard "floor diaphragm" idealization). Hence **one independent sway DOF $\Delta$** plus **two rotational DOFs $\theta_B, \theta_C$**, total DKI = 3.

## 4.4 Schematic: Slope-Deflection Sign Convention

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIGN[Slope Deflection Sign Convention - Clockwise POSITIVE]
        direction LR
        X1[Member AB] -->|M AB clockwise at A| Y1[End A]
        Y1 -->|Rotation theta A clockwise| Z1[End A]
        Y1 -->|Chord rotation psi clockwise| X1
        Y1 -->|M BA clockwise at B| X2[End B]
        X2 -->|theta B clockwise| Y2[End B]
    end
```

**Interpretation:** The KTU (and classical slope-deflection) convention takes **clockwise moments and clockwise rotations on the member end as positive**. This is critical for consistent sign handling.

## 4.5 Schematic: Shear in a Swaying Column — Force Resolution

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Column AB - base fixed - top translates by Delta] --> B[Top moves right by Delta]
    B --> C[Chord rotation psi = Delta / L clockwise]
    C --> D[M AB end moment clockwise at base]
    C --> E[M BA end moment clockwise at top]
    D --> F[Shear V AB in column resists horizontal load]
    E --> F
    F --> G[V AB = 6EI theta A + theta B - 12EI Delta over L squared plus V FEM]
```

**Interpretation:** The lateral translation of the column's top relative to its base induces a chord rotation. This chord rotation, in turn, generates end moments that combine to produce an internal shear $V_{AB}$. This shear is the column's contribution to resisting the applied lateral load $H$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Distinguish between a frame with sway and a frame without sway. Give one example of each."**

**Model Answer:**
A **frame without sway** is a rigid-jointed plane frame in which all joint translations are restrained (by supports, bracing, or by symmetry of geometry and loading), so the only kinematic unknowns are the joint rotations. *Example:* A portal frame with both supports fixed and loaded only with symmetric vertical loads; the horizontal sway is zero by symmetry.

A **frame with sway** is one in which at least one independent lateral translation (sway DOF) exists and must be treated as an additional unknown alongside the joint rotations. *Example:* A portal frame subjected to a horizontal load at the beam level, causing the entire frame to translate sideways by an unknown $\Delta$.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"What is kinematic indeterminacy? How is it computed for (i) a continuous beam and (ii) a rigid-jointed frame with sway?"**

**Model Answer:**
**Kinematic indeterminacy (DKI)** is the number of independent joint displacements (rotations + translations) that must be determined in the stiffness/displacement method of analysis.

**(i) Continuous beam:** DKI = number of internal rigid/continuous supports (since each such support has a rotation DOF, and transverse translations of all joints are zero by support conditions). For example, a 3-span continuous beam has DKI = 2.

**(ii) Rigid-jointed frame with sway:** DKI = (number of rigid joint rotations) + (number of independent sway DOFs). The sway DOFs are obtained by treating the joints as hinges and counting the minimum number of restraints needed to prevent rigid-body translation. For a single-bay, single-storey portal frame: DKI = 2 rotations + 1 sway = 3.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**"A continuous beam $ABC$ has spans $AB = 5$ m and $BC = 6$ m with a continuous support at $B$. The beam is fixed at $A$ and simply supported at $C$. A uniformly distributed load of $20$ kN/m acts over the entire length. A point load of $40$ kN acts at the midspan of $AB$. Assume constant $EI$. Using the slope-deflection / stiffness method, determine all support moments and reactions."**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — *Compute fixed-end moments and write all slope-deflection equations.*

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Fixed-End Moments (FEMs)**

For span $AB$ (length 5 m, $w = 20$ kN/m UDL):
$$FEM_{AB}^{w} = -\frac{wL^2}{12} = -\frac{20 \times 25}{12} = -41.667 \text{ kN·m}$$
$$FEM_{BA}^{w} = +\frac{20 \times 25}{12} = +41.667 \text{ kN·m}$$

For span $AB$ with central point load $P = 40$ kN:
$$FEM_{AB}^{P} = -\frac{PL}{8} = -\frac{40 \times 5}{8} = -25 \text{ kN·m}$$
$$FEM_{BA}^{P} = +\frac{PL}{8} = +25 \text{ kN·m}$$

Total FEMs for span $AB$:
$$FEM_{AB} = -41.667 - 25 = -66.667 \text{ kN·m}$$
$$FEM_{BA} = +41.667 + 25 = +66.667 \text{ kN·m}$$

For span $BC$ (length 6 m, $w = 20$ kN/m UDL):
$$FEM_{BC} = -\frac{20 \times 36}{12} = -60 \text{ kN·m}$$
$$FEM_{CB} = +60 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2: Kinematic Indeterminacy**

$\theta_A = 0$ (fixed), $\theta_C = 0$ (simply supported). Only unknown: $\theta_B$. **DKI = 1.**

**Step 3: Slope-Deflection Equations** (no chord rotation, beams only)

$$M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{5}(0) + \frac{2EI}{5}\cdot\frac{1}{2}\theta_B - 66.667 = \frac{2EI}{5}\theta_B - 66.667$$

$$M_{BA} = \frac{2EI}{5}\theta_B + 0 + 66.667 = \frac{2EI}{5}\theta_B + 66.667$$

$$M_{BC} = \frac{2EI}{6}\theta_B + 0 - 60 = \frac{EI}{3}\theta_B - 60$$

$$M_{CB} = \frac{2EI}{6}\cdot\frac{1}{2}\theta_B + 0 + 60 = \frac{EI}{6}\theta_B + 60$$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — *Apply equilibrium and compute all end moments and reactions.*

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Joint Equilibrium at B**

$$M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$$
$$\frac{2EI}{5}\theta_B + 66.667 + \frac{EI}{3}\theta_B - 60 = 0$$

Combine $\theta_B$ terms: $\frac{2}{5} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{6+5}{15} = \frac{11}{15}$

$$\frac{11EI}{15}\theta_B + 6.667 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_B = -\frac{6.667 \times 15}{11EI} = -\frac{9.091}{EI}$$

**Step 2: Substitute to Get End Moments**

$$M_{AB} = \frac{2}{5}(-9.091) - 66.667 = -3.636 - 66.667 = -70.30 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{BA} = \frac{2}{5}(-9.091) + 66.667 = -3.636 + 66.667 = +63.03 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{BC} = \frac{1}{3}(-9.091) - 60 = -3.030 - 60 = -63.03 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{CB} = \frac{1}{6}(-9.091) + 60 = -1.515 + 60 = +58.48 \text{ kN·m}$$

(Equilibrium check: $M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 63.03 - 63.03 = 0$ ✓)

**Step 3: Reactions**

For span $AB$ (load 20 kN/m UDL + 40 kN central point, $L=5$, $M_A = -70.30$, $M_B = +63.03$):
- $\sum M_A = 0$: $V_B \times 5 - 20\times5\times2.5 - 40\times2.5 + 70.30 - 63.03 = 0$
- $5V_B - 250 - 100 + 7.27 = 0 \Rightarrow V_B = 68.55$ kN
- $V_A = (20 \times 5 + 40) - 68.55 = 140 - 68.55 = 71.45$ kN

For span $BC$ (load 20 kN/m UDL, $L=6$, $M_B = -63.03$, $M_C = +58.48$):
- $\sum M_B = 0$: $V_C \times 6 - 20\times6\times3 + (-63.03) - 58.48 = 0$
- $6V_C - 360 - 121.51 = 0 \Rightarrow V_C = 80.25$ kN
- $V_B^{(BC)} = 120 - 80.25 = 39.75$ kN

**Total reactions:**
- $R_A = V_A = 71.45$ kN ↑
- $R_B = V_B^{(AB)} + V_B^{(BC)} = 68.55 + 39.75 = 108.30$ kN ↑
- $R_C = V_C = 80.25$ kN ↑

(Check: $71.45 + 108.30 + 80.25 = 260$ kN; total load = $20\times11 + 40 = 220 + 40 = 260$ kN ✓)

---

### Question Choice B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"A portal frame $ABCD$ consists of two columns $AB$ and $CD$ of length 4 m each, and a horizontal beam $BC$ of length 6 m. All members have flexural rigidity $EI$ (constant). The frame is fixed at $A$ and $D$. A horizontal force of 30 kN is applied at joint $B$ from the left. Using the stiffness method, determine all member-end moments. Show clearly how the sway DOF is handled."**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — *Identify DOFs, write slope-deflection equations, and set up the joint equilibrium equations.*

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Kinematic Indeterminacy**

- $\theta_B, \theta_C$: joint rotations.
- $\Delta$: sway at the beam level (single sway DOF, assuming rigid beam, so $B$ and $C$ translate by the same $\Delta$).
- $\theta_A = \theta_D = 0$ (fixed supports).
- **Total DKI = 3.**

**Step 2: Chord Rotations**

- Columns $AB$, $CD$: $\psi = \Delta/4$ (positive $\Delta$ to the right induces a clockwise chord rotation of both columns — positive by sign convention).
- Beam $BC$: chord rotation = 0 (both ends translate vertically by the same amount; no relative transverse displacement, hence $\psi = 0$).

**Step 3: Slope-Deflection Equations** (no FEMs since loads are joint loads, not member loads)

**Column $AB$:**
$$M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{4}\left(0 + \theta_B - \frac{3\Delta}{4}\right) = \frac{EI}{2}\theta_B - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta$$

$$M_{BA} = \frac{2EI}{4}\left(2\theta_B + 0 - \frac{3\Delta}{4}\right) = EI\,\theta_B - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta$$

**Column $CD$:**
$$M_{DC} = \frac{EI}{2}\theta_C - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta$$

$$M_{CD} = EI\,\theta_C - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta$$

**Beam $BC$:**
$$M_{BC} = \frac{2EI}{6}\left(2\theta_B + \theta_C\right) = \frac{2EI}{3}\theta_B + \frac{EI}{3}\theta_C$$

$$M_{CB} = \frac{2EI}{6}\left(2\theta_C + \theta_B\right) = \frac{EI}{3}\theta_B + \frac{2EI}{3}\theta_C$$

**Step 4: Joint Equilibrium**

At $B$: $M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$

$$EI\,\theta_B - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta + \frac{2EI}{3}\theta_B + \frac{EI}{3}\theta_C = 0$$

Dividing by $EI$:

$$\frac{5}{3}\theta_B + \frac{1}{3}\theta_C - \frac{3}{8}\Delta = 0 \quad \cdots (1)$$

At $C$: $M_{CB} + M_{CD} = 0$

$$\frac{EI}{3}\theta_B + \frac{2EI}{3}\theta_C + EI\,\theta_C - \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{3}\theta_B + \frac{5}{3}\theta_C - \frac{3}{8}\Delta = 0 \quad \cdots (2)$$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — *Set up the shear equation, solve the system, and compute all end moments.*

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Shear Equation (Sway Equilibrium)**

The applied horizontal force is $H = 30$ kN at joint $B$ (acting to the right). The total horizontal reaction from the columns must balance this. The shear in each column (acting at the top) is $V_{AB}$ and $V_{DC}$:

$$V_{AB} = -\frac{M_{AB} + M_{BA}}{4} = -\frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{3EI}{2}\theta_B - \frac{3EI}{4}\Delta\right] = -\frac{3EI}{8}\theta_B + \frac{3EI}{16}\Delta$$

$$V_{DC} = -\frac{M_{DC} + M_{CD}}{4} = -\frac{3EI}{8}\theta_C + \frac{3EI}{16}\Delta$$

Summing horizontal forces on the entire frame (sum of column shears = applied horizontal force):

$$V_{AB} + V_{DC} = 30$$

$$-\frac{3EI}{8}(\theta_B + \theta_C) + \frac{3EI}{8}\Delta = 30 \quad \cdots (3)$$

**Step 2: Solve the System**

Equations (1), (2), (3) form a 3×3 linear system in $\theta_B, \theta_C, \Delta$.

Subtract (2) from (1):
$$\frac{4}{3}(\theta_B - \theta_C) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_B = \theta_C$$

(This is the anti-symmetric sway mode, expected by symmetry of geometry.)

Let $\theta_B = \theta_C = \theta$. Substituting into (1):

$$\frac{5}{3}\theta + \frac{1}{3}\theta - \frac{3}{8}\Delta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2\theta = \frac{3}{8}\Delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac{3\Delta}{16}$$

Wait — by anti-symmetry, if the load is to the right at $B$, the rotations should be anti-symmetric: $\theta_C = -\theta_B$. Let us re-examine.

For a horizontal load $H$ at the beam level, the sway mode is **anti-symmetric**: the frame leans to the right, joint $B$ rotates one way and joint $C$ rotates the opposite way. Hence $\theta_C = -\theta_B$.

Re-examine: with $\theta_C = -\theta_B$:

Equation (1): $\frac{5}{3}\theta_B - \frac{1}{3}\theta_B - \frac{3}{8}\Delta = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{4}{3}\theta_B = \frac{3}{8}\Delta \Rightarrow \theta_B = \frac{9}{32}\Delta$

Equation (3): $-\frac{3}{8}(0) + \frac{3}{8}\Delta = 30 \Rightarrow \Delta = 80/EI$ (taking $EI$ constant)

Therefore:
$$\theta_B = \frac{9}{32} \cdot \frac{80}{EI} = \frac{22.5}{EI}, \quad \theta_C = -\frac{22.5}{EI}, \quad \Delta = \frac{80}{EI}$$

**Step 3: Compute End Moments**

Column $AB$:
$$M_{AB} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot 22.5 - \frac{3}{8}\cdot 80 = 11.25 - 30 = -18.75 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{BA} = 22.5 - 30 = -7.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

Column $CD$ (anti-symmetric):
$$M_{DC} = -\frac{1}{2}\cdot 22.5 + \frac{3}{8}\cdot 80 = -11.25 + 30 = +18.75 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{CD} = -22.5 + 30 = +7.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

Beam $BC$:
$$M_{BC} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot 22.5 + \frac{1}{3}\cdot(-22.5) = 15 - 7.5 = +7.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{CB} = \frac{1}{3}\cdot 22.5 + \frac{2}{3}\cdot(-22.5) = 7.5 - 15 = -7.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Verification:** Joint $B$: $M_{BA} + M_{BC} = -7.5 + 7.5 = 0$ ✓ Joint $C$: $M_{CB} + M_{CD} = -7.5 + 7.5 = 0$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Losers:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting that chord rotation appears as $-3\psi$ (with the negative sign) in the slope-deflection equation. Many students write $+3\psi$, leading to wrong moment signs.
> 2. **Missing the shear equation (–3 to –4 marks):** In a sway frame, students often write only the moment equilibrium equations (one per joint) and forget the **shear equation** derived from horizontal force balance. This gives an under-determined system and the answer is marked wrong.
> 3. **Wrong chord rotation (–2 marks):** Setting $\psi = \Delta/L$ for the horizontal beam (it should be **zero**, since the beam ends translate horizontally by the same amount and don't rotate relative to each other).
> 4. **Algebraic slip in $4EI/L$ vs $2EI/L$ (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing up the rotational stiffness coefficient (4EI/L) with the carry-over term (2EI/L).
> 5. **No verification (–1 mark):** KTU examiners always check the equilibrium check. Always verify $\sum M_{\text{joint}} = 0$ at every rigid joint as a final step.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Master List for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Slope-Deflection Equation (master form):**
$$M_{AB} = \frac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi) + FEM_{AB}$$
- **Chord Rotation:** $\psi = \Delta/L$ for columns; **zero** for beams (no relative transverse displacement of beam ends under rigid-floor sway).
- **Sign Convention:** Clockwise moments and clockwise rotations on the **member end** are **POSITIVE**.
- **Stiffness Coefficients (for a prismatic beam):**
  - Diagonal: $K_{ii} = 4EI/L$ (single member); $K_{ii} = \sum 4EI/L$ at a joint where multiple members meet.
  - Off-diagonal: $K_{ij} = 2EI/L$.
- **Continuous Beam:** DKI = number of internal continuous supports. No sway equation needed.
- **Frame Without Sway:** Same as continuous beam but with multiple members per joint. $\sum M_{\text{joint}} = 0$ at every rigid joint.
- **Frame With Sway:** 
  - 1. $\sum M = 0$ at every rigid joint.
  - 2. $\sum H = 0$ for the whole frame (one independent shear equation per sway DOF).
  - 3. Column shear: $V = -\frac{M_{top} + M_{bottom}}{L}$.
- **Fixed-End Moments (must memorize):**
  - UDL: $\pm wL^2/12$ (left end negative, right end positive).
  - Central point load: $\pm PL/8$.
  - Point load at distance $a$ from $A$, $b$ from $B$: $FEM_{AB} = -Pab^2/L^2$, $FEM_{BA} = +Pa^2b/L^2$.
- **Anti-Symmetry Shortcut:** For symmetric frames with anti-symmetric loading (lateral loads), $\theta_C = -\theta_B$ — this eliminates one unknown.
- **Final Check:** Always verify joint equilibrium $\sum M = 0$ at every joint and shear balance for sway frames.
- **SI Units:** $EI$ in kN·m², $L$ in m, $M$ in kN·m, $\theta$ in rad, $\Delta$ in m.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Support Settlement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST403 - ANALYSIS OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Support Settlement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Effect of Support Settlement** is a modification applied to the classical **Slope-Deflection Method** of structural analysis to account for displacements (vertical translations) at the supports of a continuous beam or frame. When a support of a structure settles by an amount $\Delta$ (due to soil consolidation, foundation failure, mining subsidence, or differential loading of footings), the member chords rotate, generating **fixed end moments (FEM)** even when there is **no external load** on the member.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Support settlement induces *geometric displacement* of a member's chord, which is kinematically equivalent to an *applied rotation* $\psi = \dfrac{\Delta}{L}$ at the member ends. The Slope-Deflection equations must therefore be reformulated to include this *chord rotation* term $\psi$.

The mathematical statement of the modified Slope-Deflection Equation is:

$$M_{AB} = M^{F}_{AB} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_{A} + \theta_{B} - 3\psi\right)$$

$$M_{BA} = M^{F}_{BA} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_{B} + \theta_{A} - 3\psi\right)$$

where $\psi$ is the **chord rotation** (also called the *settlement rotation* or *drift*) of the member.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real World Picture

Imagine you place a **wooden plank** across two bricks. If you carefully remove a small wooden wedge from under one brick, that brick sinks down a little. The plank, although still resting on the bricks, now tilts. Even though you did not apply any new weight on the plank, it bends, and if you measure the internal stresses using a strain gauge, you will see **non-zero bending moments**. This is precisely what happens in a continuous beam when one of its supports settles:

- The **plank** = the structural member (beam/column).
- The **brick that sinks** = the support that experiences settlement $\Delta$.
- The **tilt of the plank** = the *chord rotation* $\psi$.
- The **internal bending stress** in the plank = the **induced fixed end moment** $M^{F}$ due to settlement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Engineering Context:** Settlement-induced moments are critical in the design of bridges, multi-storey buildings on soft alluvial soil, flyovers, and viaducts. The Koynanagar and Chenab Bridge projects in India incorporated settlement analysis as a primary design check. **Typical tolerable settlement** in framed structures is **$\mathbf{20\text{ mm}}$** to **$\mathbf{40\text{ mm}}$** as per IS 1904.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Highlights)

- **Settlement $\Delta$**: Vertical downward displacement of a support, measured in **mm** or **m**.
- **Chord Rotation $\psi = \Delta / L$**: Dimensionless (radians), treated as a *kinematic input* in the Slope-Deflection equation.
- **Modified Fixed End Moment (due to settlement)**: $M^{F}_{AB, \text{settle}} = \mp \dfrac{6EI\Delta}{L^{2}}$, sign convention based on chord rotation direction.
- **Flexural Rigidity $EI$**: Constant for a prismatic member, units **$\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$** or **$\text{N}\cdot\text{mm}^{2}$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The classical Slope-Deflection method was originally developed assuming **immovable (fixed-in-position) supports**. To extend it for *movable supports*, the analysis proceeds in the following logical steps:

**Step 1 — Identify Settlement at Each Support**
- For every joint, list the vertical settlement $\Delta_A, \Delta_B, \Delta_C, \ldots$
- Compute the **relative settlement** between the ends of each member:
$$\delta_{AB} = \Delta_B - \Delta_A$$
- Convert this to **chord rotation**:
$$\psi_{AB} = \frac{\delta_{AB}}{L_{AB}}$$

**Step 2 — Superpose Settlement Effect on External Load Effect**
- The principle of superposition is valid for linearly elastic structures. Thus:
$$M^{F}_{AB,\text{total}} = M^{F}_{AB,\text{load}} + M^{F}_{AB,\text{settlement}}$$
- The **settlement-only FEM** is:
$$M^{F}_{AB} = -\frac{6EI_{AB}}{L_{AB}}\psi_{AB}, \qquad M^{F}_{BA} = +\frac{6EI_{AB}}{L_{AB}}\psi_{AB}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Modified Slope-Deflection Equation**
- The general equation is reformulated as:
$$M_{AB} = M^{F}_{AB} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi_{AB}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Impose Equilibrium**
- **Joint Equilibrium**: $\sum M_{\text{joint}} = 0$ at every rigid joint.
- **Shear / Story Equilibrium**: $\sum M = 0$ for the entire beam (to handle sway/settlement in frames).

**Step 5 — Solve the System**
- Solve the simultaneous linear equations for unknown joint rotations $\theta$ and the new unknown chord rotations $\psi$ (when support settlement is prescribed, $\psi$ becomes a known input, simplifying the system).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Sl. No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Sign Convention / Notes** |
|:-----------:|:-------------|:------------|:----------------------------|
| 1 | Relative Settlement | $\delta_{AB} = \Delta_B - \Delta_A$ | Positive if B settles more than A |
| 2 | Chord Rotation | $\psi_{AB} = \dfrac{\delta_{AB}}{L_{AB}}$ | Clockwise (about right end) is **positive** |
| 3 | Settlement FEM (left end) | $M^{F}_{AB} = -\dfrac{6EI}{L}\,\psi_{AB}$ | Anticlockwise on member at A is **positive** |
| 4 | Settlement FEM (right end) | $M^{F}_{BA} = +\dfrac{6EI}{L}\,\psi_{AB}$ | Clockwise on member at B is **positive** |
| 5 | Modified Slope-Deflection | $M_{AB} = M^{F}_{AB} - \dfrac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi_{AB}\right)$ | Standard KTU convention |
| 6 | Modified Slope-Deflection (B end) | $M_{BA} = M^{F}_{BA} - \dfrac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_B + \theta_A - 3\psi_{AB}\right)$ | Symmetric form |
| 7 | Total FEM (load + settlement) | $M^{F}_{\text{total}} = M^{F}_{\text{load}} + M^{F}_{\text{settle}}$ | Superposition principle |
| 8 | Sign of $\psi$ in 3-term | $\psi$ is positive when chord rotates **clockwise** | Check: B drops below A $\Rightarrow$ $\psi$ positive |
| 9 | Joint Equilibrium | $\sum M_{\text{joint}} = 0$ | Applied to every rigid joint |
| 10 | Modified Stiffness Term | Effective coefficient of $\theta$ is **$4EI/L$** | Settlement does not change stiffness, only adds geometric term |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The most common error is **forgetting to add the settlement FEM** to the load FEM. Always compute both contributions and add them algebraically before substituting into the slope-deflection equation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Effect of Support Settlement analysis is indispensable in:

1. **Bridge Engineering** — Differential settlement between piers and abutments can redistribute moments by **30–60%** in continuous box girder bridges.
2. **Tall Building Design on Soft Soil** — Settlement of pile caps in deep alluvial deposits (e.g., Kerala's coastal *karimeen* belt) can introduce unexpected moments in transfer beams and pile caps.
3. **Industrial Structures with Crane Girders** — Differential settlement of column footings causes crane runway misalignment, which is mitigated only by predicting settlement-induced moments.
4. **Dam and Hydro Structures** — Sluice gate piers settle due to scour, and the induced moments must be checked in the design of the deck slab.
5. **Historical Building Restoration** — Ancient structures (e.g., Kerala temple *gopurams*) often have uneven settlement; retrofitting requires quantifying the moments already induced.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Modified Slope-Deflection Equation from First Principles

We begin with the **conjugate beam / unit load analogue** reasoning. For a prismatic beam AB of length $L$ with both ends initially fixed, the ends are then given:
- A rotation $\theta_A$ at A (clockwise positive on member)
- A rotation $\theta_B$ at B (clockwise positive on member)
- A **rigid body chord rotation** $\psi$ (clockwise positive, equivalent to A moving up by $\Delta_A$ and B moving down by $\Delta_B$, with $\psi = (\Delta_B - \Delta_A)/L$).

The total relative rotation between the chord and the tangent at end A is $( \theta_A - \psi )$, and at end B is $( \theta_B - \psi )$.

The **bending moment at end A** of a beam undergoing these three kinematical actions (using the slope-deflection stiffness coefficients) is the sum of:
- Moment due to rotation $\theta_A$ at A: $\dfrac{4EI}{L}\,\theta_A$
- Moment due to rotation $\theta_B$ at B: $\dfrac{2EI}{L}\,\theta_B$
- Moment due to chord rotation $\psi$: $-\dfrac{6EI}{L}\,\psi$ (this is the *carry-over* effect of chord rotation acting like a moment at both ends)

Adding the fixed end moment $M^{F}_{AB}$ (which is zero for pure settlement case if no loads exist, but non-zero if loads coexist):

$$M_{AB} = M^{F}_{AB} + \frac{4EI}{L}\theta_A + \frac{2EI}{L}\theta_B - \frac{6EI}{L}\psi$$

Regrouping terms to obtain the canonical slope-deflection form:

$$M_{AB} = M^{F}_{AB} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi\right)$$

This is the **canonical modified slope-deflection equation** demanded by KTU. The procedure to obtain $M_{BA}$ is identical by symmetry of the coefficients.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Settlement-Only FEM for a Propped Cantilever

**Problem:** A beam AB of length $L = 6\,\text{m}$, flexural rigidity $EI = 12{,}000\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$, is fixed at A and simply supported at B. Support B settles vertically downward by $\Delta = 12\,\text{mm} = 0.012\,\text{m}$. Determine the moment at the fixed end A.

**Step 1 — Sign Convention Setup**
Settlement of B below A makes the chord rotate **clockwise** about A, so $\psi$ is **positive**:

$$\psi = \frac{\Delta_B - \Delta_A}{L} = \frac{0.012 - 0}{6} = +0.002\,\text{rad}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Slope-Deflection Equation at A**
At A, the joint rotation is $\theta_A = 0$ (fixed support). At B, we are using a *propped* support, but for a fully fixed-fixed case the FEM is directly:

$$M_{AB} = M^{F}_{AB} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions**
For the *fully fixed-fixed* case (which is the basis for computing settlement FEM):

$$M^{F}_{AB,\text{settle}} = -\frac{6EI}{L}\psi$$

Substituting the values:

$$M^{F}_{AB} = -\frac{6 \times 12{,}000 \times 0.002}{6}$$

Evaluating the numerator step-by-step:

$$6 \times 12{,}000 = 72{,}000$$

$$72{,}000 \times 0.002 = 144$$

$$M^{F}_{AB} = -\frac{144}{6} = -24\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute FEM at B (right end)**

$$M^{F}_{BA} = +\frac{6EI}{L}\psi = +24\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium Check for Fixed-Fixed Beam**
The two fixed end moments must be equal and opposite to keep the beam in equilibrium:

$$M^{F}_{AB} + M^{F}_{BA} = -24 + 24 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A settlement of merely **12 mm** in a **6 m** span induced a moment of **$24\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$** at the fixed support — equivalent to the moment produced by a UDL of roughly **$\mathbf{8\,\text{kN/m}}$** on a fixed-fixed beam of the same span! This shows why support settlement is a *first-order* design consideration.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Three-Span Continuous Beam with Settlement

Consider a beam ABCD with three equal spans $L$ each, where support B settles by $\Delta$ and supports A, C, D do not settle. The Slope-Deflection equations for each member (with appropriate $\psi$) become:

**Member AB** (A is fixed, so $\theta_A = 0$):

$$M_{AB} = M^{F}_{AB} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(\theta_B - 3\psi_{AB}\right)$$

$$M_{BA} = M^{F}_{BA} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_B - 3\psi_{AB}\right)$$

where $\psi_{AB} = \dfrac{-\Delta}{L}$ (B settles down relative to A, so chord rotates *anticlockwise* about A from A's perspective — careful sign audit needed).

**Member BC** (no relative settlement assumed, $\psi_{BC} = 0$):

$$M_{BC} = M^{F}_{BC} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_B + \theta_C\right)$$

$$M_{CB} = M^{F}_{CB} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_C + \theta_B\right)$$

**Joint Equilibrium Equations:**

$$\sum M_B = 0: \quad M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$$

$$\sum M_C = 0: \quad M_{CB} + M_{CD} = 0$$

These two equations in two unknowns ($\theta_B, \theta_C$) yield a $2 \times 2$ linear system that is solvable by Cramer's rule or matrix inversion — the **stiffness matrix** approach used in KTU's Module 3.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Settlement Analysis Workflow

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the complete procedure for analyzing the Effect of Support Settlement using the Slope-Deflection method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Structure Geometry] --> B[Identify All Supports and Span Lengths L]
    B --> C[Record Settlement Values Delta at Each Support]
    C --> D[Compute Relative Settlement delta_eq for Each Member]
    D --> E[Compute Chord Rotation psi_eq = delta_eq divided by L]
    E --> F[Compute Fixed End Moments due to External Loads]
    F --> G[Compute Settlement FEM: minus 6EI psi divided by L]
    G --> H[Sum FEM_total_eq = FEM_load + FEM_settle]
    H --> I[Write Modified Slope-Deflection Equations for Each Member]
    I --> J[Apply Joint Equilibrium: sum of M at each joint equals zero]
    J --> K[Solve Simultaneous Linear Equations for Unknown Rotations theta]
    K --> L[Back-Substitute theta into Slope-Deflection Equations]
    L --> M[Obtain Final End Moments M_AB, M_BA for Each Member]
    M --> N[Perform Static Equilibrium Check: sum of M and V equals zero]
    N --> O[Plot Bending Moment Diagram with Settlement Effect]
    O --> P[End: Design Moments for Beam and Connections]
```

### 4.2 Free-Body Schematic of a Beam with One Support Settlement

The following Mermaid diagram represents a **fixed-fixed beam AB** with the right support B settling by an amount $\Delta$. The induced fixed end moments and chord rotation are labelled.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[A: Fixed Support] -->|Length L| B[B: Fixed Support with Settlement Delta]
    A -->|M_FAB_eq minus 6EI Delta over L squared| A
    B -->|M_FBA_eq plus 6EI Delta over L squared| B
    A -.->|Chord Rotates by psi_eq equals Delta over L| B
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Direction of Chord Rotation $\psi$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Original Position: Horizontal Beam AB] --> S2[B Settles Down by Delta]
    S2 --> S3[Chord AB Tilted Clockwise by angle psi]
    S3 --> S4[Equivalent Kinematic: A is lifted up by Delta relative to B]
    S4 --> S5[Sign Convention: psi is positive when chord rotates clockwise]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the Mermaid block above, every box represents a *process step* and every arrow represents a *data flow*. The notation `delta_eq`, `psi_eq`, and `FEM` follows the standard KTU Slope-Deflection nomenclature, where subscript `_eq` indicates the equilibrium/computed value.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define chord rotation in the slope-deflection method. How is it related to support settlement?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Chord rotation $\psi$ is the angle through which the chord of a deformed member rotates from its original (undeformed) position, measured about a horizontal axis. In the presence of support settlement, the chord of a member rotates rigidly due to the relative vertical displacement between its two ends. The mathematical relation is:

$$\psi = \frac{\Delta_B - \Delta_A}{L}$$

where $\Delta_A$ and $\Delta_B$ are the vertical settlements of the two supports and $L$ is the span. It is measured positive when the chord rotates clockwise. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for sign convention and physical meaning.)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Write the modified slope-deflection equation including the effect of support settlement and explain the additional term.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The modified slope-deflection equation for member AB is:

$$M_{AB} = M^{F}_{AB} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi\right)$$

The term $3\psi$ in the bracket represents the effect of chord rotation (support settlement) on the end moment. The induced settlement fixed end moments are $M^{F}_{AB} = -6EI\psi/L$ and $M^{F}_{BA} = +6EI\psi/L$. **(1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for explanation of $3\psi$ term, 1 mark for settlement FEM expressions.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
> A continuous beam ABC of two equal spans, each of length $L = 5\,\text{m}$, has a uniform flexural rigidity $EI = 8{,}000\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$. The ends A and C are simply supported, and joint B is a continuous interior support. A uniformly distributed load of intensity $w = 12\,\text{kN/m}$ acts over the entire beam. The support at B settles vertically downward by $\Delta = 10\,\text{mm}$. Using the slope-deflection method, determine:
> **(a)** The fixed end moments for both spans including the settlement effect. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** The final end moments at supports A, B, and C. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Given Data:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 5\,\text{m} \\
EI &= 8{,}000\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}^{2} \\
w &= 12\,\text{kN/m} \\
\Delta_B &= 10\,\text{mm} = 0.010\,\text{m}, \quad \Delta_A = \Delta_C = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (a) — Fixed End Moments (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Load-only Fixed End Moments**
For a UDL on a fixed-fixed beam, $M^{F}_{\text{load}} = \pm wL^{2}/12$:

$$M^{F}_{AB,\text{load}} = -\frac{wL^{2}}{12} = -\frac{12 \times 5^{2}}{12} = -25\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

$$M^{F}_{BA,\text{load}} = +\frac{wL^{2}}{12} = +25\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

(Same values for span BC.)

**Step 2 — Chord Rotation for Each Span**

For span AB, B settles below A:

$$\psi_{AB} = \frac{\Delta_B - \Delta_A}{L} = \frac{0.010 - 0}{5} = +0.002\,\text{rad}$$

For span BC, C does not settle and B settles:

$$\psi_{BC} = \frac{\Delta_C - \Delta_B}{L} = \frac{0 - 0.010}{5} = -0.002\,\text{rad}$$

**Step 3 — Settlement-only Fixed End Moments**

For span AB:

$$M^{F}_{AB,\text{settle}} = -\frac{6EI}{L}\psi_{AB} = -\frac{6 \times 8{,}000 \times 0.002}{5} = -19.2\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

$$M^{F}_{BA,\text{settle}} = +\frac{6EI}{L}\psi_{AB} = +19.2\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

For span BC:

$$M^{F}_{BC,\text{settle}} = -\frac{6EI}{L}\psi_{BC} = -\frac{6 \times 8{,}000 \times (-0.002)}{5} = +19.2\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

$$M^{F}_{CB,\text{settle}} = +\frac{6EI}{L}\psi_{BC} = \frac{6 \times 8{,}000 \times (-0.002)}{5} = -19.2\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Total Fixed End Moments**

$$\begin{aligned}
M^{F}_{AB} &= -25 + (-19.2) = -44.2\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m} \\
M^{F}_{BA} &= +25 + 19.2 = +44.2\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m} \\
M^{F}_{BC} &= -25 + 19.2 = -5.8\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m} \\
M^{F}_{CB} &= +25 + (-19.2) = +5.8\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(Part (a) complete: All four FEM values tabulated and derived.)**

**Part (b) — Final End Moments (7 Marks)**

**Step 5 — Slope-Deflection Equations**
Since A and C are simple supports, the end moments $M_{AB}$ and $M_{CB}$ must be **zero** (free rotation, no restraint). Therefore, we use these as **release conditions** to find $\theta_B$.

The slope-deflection equation at B (end A of span AB):

$$M_{BA} = M^{F}_{BA} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_B + \theta_A - 3\psi_{AB}\right)$$

With $\theta_A = 0$:

$$M_{BA} = 44.2 - \frac{2 \times 8{,}000}{5}\left(2\theta_B - 3 \times 0.002\right)$$

$$M_{BA} = 44.2 - 3{,}200\left(2\theta_B - 0.006\right)$$

$$M_{BA} = 44.2 - 6{,}400\,\theta_B + 19.2 = 63.4 - 6{,}400\,\theta_B$$

The slope-deflection equation at B (end B of span BC):

$$M_{BC} = M^{F}_{BC} - \frac{2EI}{L}\left(2\theta_B + \theta_C - 3\psi_{BC}\right)$$

With $\theta_C = 0$:

$$M_{BC} = -5.8 - 3{,}200\left(2\theta_B - 3 \times (-0.002)\right)$$

$$M_{BC} = -5.8 - 6{,}400\,\theta_B - 19.2 = -25.0 - 6{,}400\,\theta_B$$

**Step 6 — Joint Equilibrium at B**

$$\sum M_B = M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$$

$$(63.4 - 6{,}400\,\theta_B) + (-25.0 - 6{,}400\,\theta_B) = 0$$

$$38.4 - 12{,}800\,\theta_B = 0$$

$$\theta_B = \frac{38.4}{12{,}800} = +0.003\,\text{rad}$$

**Step 7 — Back-Substitute to Find End Moments**

$$M_{BA} = 63.4 - 6{,}400 \times 0.003 = 63.4 - 19.2 = +44.2\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

$$M_{BC} = -25.0 - 6{,}400 \times 0.003 = -25.0 - 19.2 = -44.2\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

$$M_{AB} = 0, \quad M_{CB} = 0 \quad \text{(simple supports)}$$

**Verification (Joint Equilibrium at B):**
$M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 44.2 - 44.2 = 0$ ✓

**Final Answer for Question A:**

$$\boxed{M_{AB} = 0, \quad M_{BA} = +44.2\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}, \quad M_{BC} = -44.2\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}, \quad M_{CB} = 0}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Alternative Set]**
> A two-span continuous beam ABC has spans $AB = 4\,\text{m}$ and $BC = 6\,\text{m}$, with $EI$ constant throughout. A central point load of $40\,\text{kN}$ acts at the midspan of AB. Support B settles by $\Delta = 8\,\text{mm}$ while A and C do not settle. Assume $EI = 10{,}000\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$. Using the slope-deflection method:
> **(a)** Compute the modified fixed end moments (load + settlement) for both spans. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Determine the final end moments and sketch the bending moment diagram. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Given Data:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_1 = L_{AB} &= 4\,\text{m}, \quad L_2 = L_{BC} = 6\,\text{m} \\
P &= 40\,\text{kN at midspan of AB} \\
\Delta_B &= 8\,\text{mm} = 0.008\,\text{m}, \quad \Delta_A = \Delta_C = 0 \\
EI &= 10{,}000\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (a) — Modified Fixed End Moments (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Load-only FEM for Span AB** (central point load $P$):

$$M^{F}_{AB,\text{load}} = -\frac{PL}{8} = -\frac{40 \times 4}{8} = -20\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

$$M^{F}_{BA,\text{load}} = +\frac{PL}{8} = +20\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Load-only FEM for Span BC** (no load, so zero):

$$M^{F}_{BC,\text{load}} = 0, \quad M^{F}_{CB,\text{load}} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Chord Rotations**

$$\psi_{AB} = \frac{\Delta_B - \Delta_A}{L_{AB}} = \frac{0.008}{4} = +0.002\,\text{rad}$$

$$\psi_{BC} = \frac{\Delta_C - \Delta_B}{L_{BC}} = \frac{-0.008}{6} = -0.00133\,\text{rad}$$

**Step 4 — Settlement-only FEMs**

For span AB:

$$M^{F}_{AB,\text{settle}} = -\frac{6EI}{L_{AB}}\psi_{AB} = -\frac{6 \times 10{,}000 \times 0.002}{4} = -30\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

$$M^{F}_{BA,\text{settle}} = +\frac{6EI}{L_{AB}}\psi_{AB} = +30\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

For span BC:

$$M^{F}_{BC,\text{settle}} = -\frac{6EI}{L_{BC}}\psi_{BC} = -\frac{6 \times 10{,}000 \times (-0.00133)}{6} = +13.33\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

$$M^{F}_{CB,\text{settle}} = +\frac{6EI}{L_{BC}}\psi_{BC} = -13.33\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Total FEMs (Load + Settlement)**

$$\begin{aligned}
M^{F}_{AB} &= -20 + (-30) = -50\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m} \\
M^{F}_{BA} &= +20 + 30 = +50\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m} \\
M^{F}_{BC} &= 0 + 13.33 = +13.33\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m} \\
M^{F}_{CB} &= 0 - 13.33 = -13.33\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(Part (a) complete.)**

**Part (b) — Final End Moments and BMD (7 Marks)**

**Step 6 — Slope-Deflection Equations**
A and C are simple supports, so $\theta_A = \theta_C = 0$ and $M_{AB} = M_{CB} = 0$ as boundary conditions.

For member AB, using $M_{AB} = 0$ as a condition:

$$0 = M^{F}_{AB} - \frac{2EI}{L_{AB}}\left(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi_{AB}\right)$$

$$0 = -50 - \frac{2 \times 10{,}000}{4}\left(0 + \theta_B - 0.006\right)$$

$$0 = -50 - 5{,}000\left(\theta_B - 0.006\right)$$

$$0 = -50 - 5{,}000\,\theta_B + 30$$

$$5{,}000\,\theta_B = -20 \Rightarrow \theta_B = -0.004\,\text{rad}$$

**Step 7 — Compute $M_{BA}$ Using the Modified Equation**

$$M_{BA} = M^{F}_{BA} - \frac{2EI}{L_{AB}}\left(2\theta_B - 3\psi_{AB}\right)$$

$$M_{BA} = 50 - 5{,}000 \times \left(2 \times (-0.004) - 0.006\right)$$

$$M_{BA} = 50 - 5{,}000 \times (-0.008 - 0.006)$$

$$M_{BA} = 50 - 5{,}000 \times (-0.014) = 50 + 70 = +120\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

**Step 8 — Compute $M_{BC}$ Using Joint Equilibrium**

At joint B: $M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$ (no external moment applied):

$$M_{BC} = -M_{BA} = -120\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$$

**Verification by direct substitution:**

$$M_{BC} = M^{F}_{BC} - \frac{2EI}{L_{BC}}\left(2\theta_B - 3\psi_{BC}\right)$$

$$M_{BC} = 13.33 - \frac{2 \times 10{,}000}{6}\left(2 \times (-0.004) - 3 \times (-0.00133)\right)$$

$$M_{BC} = 13.33 - 3{,}333.33 \times (-0.008 + 0.004)$$

$$M_{BC} = 13.33 - 3{,}333.33 \times (-0.004) = 13.33 + 13.33 = 26.66$$

This doesn't match $-120$ — there is an inconsistency because we cannot simultaneously satisfy $M_{AB}=0$ and the joint equilibrium $M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$ for this statically indeterminate case. The correct approach is to **solve the joint equilibrium equation directly without using the simple-end condition prematurely**. 

**Corrected Step 6 (Joint Equilibrium Approach):**

The slope-deflection equations with the simple-end conditions $M_{AB} = M_{CB} = 0$ substituted back give **only one equation** for $\theta_B$ from each span, and these must be **consistent with joint equilibrium**. The proper method is:

For span AB (using $M_{AB} = 0$ as a release):

$$M_{AB} = 0 = -50 - 5{,}000(\theta_B - 0.006) \Rightarrow \theta_B = -0.004\,\text{rad}$$

For span BC (using $M_{CB} = 0$ as a release):

$$M_{CB} = 0 = -13.33 - \frac{2 \times 10{,}000}{6}\left(2\theta_C + \theta_B - 3\psi_{BC}\right)$$

With $\theta_C = 0$:

$$0 = -13.33 - 3{,}333.33\left(\theta_B + 0.004\right)$$

$$3{,}333.33\,\theta_B = -13.33 - 13.33 = -26.66$$

$$\theta_B = -0.008\,\text{rad}$$

**Conflict Resolution:** The two values of $\theta_B$ disagree, which means the problem is **statically indeterminate with one redundancy**, requiring the joint equilibrium $M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$ as the **compatibility condition**. We must therefore use a different strategy:

Write $M_{BA}$ and $M_{BC}$ as functions of $\theta_B$ (keeping the equations unsimplified):

$$M_{BA} = 50 - 5{,}000(2\theta_B - 0.006) = 80 - 10{,}000\,\theta_B$$

$$M_{BC} = 13.33 - 3{,}333.33(2\theta_B + 0.004) = 13.33 - 6{,}666.67\,\theta_B - 13.33 = -6{,}666.67\,\theta_B$$

Apply $M_{BA} + M_{BC} = 0$:

$$80 - 10{,}000\,\theta_B - 6{,}666.67\,\theta_B = 0$$

$$80 = 16{,}666.67\,\theta_B \Rightarrow \theta_B = +0.0048\,\text{rad}$$

**Final End Moments:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{BA} &= 80 - 10{,}000 \times 0.0048 = 80 - 48 = +32\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m} \\
M_{BC} &= -6{,}666.67 \times 0.0048 = -32\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m} \\
M_{AB} &= 0, \quad M_{CB} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** apply the simple-end release equation twice independently — this gives inconsistent results. The correct procedure is to write **all slope-deflection equations in terms of $\theta_B$** and then apply **joint equilibrium** as the single equation.
> 2. Always verify **sign convention** for $\psi$: if the *right* support of a span settles more than the left, $\psi$ is positive (clockwise chord rotation).
> 3. The settlement FEM $\pm 6EI\psi/L$ is **independent** of the applied load FEM — they are *superposed* at the end.
> 4. In the final answer, **retain 2 decimal places** for moments in kN·m; KTU marks are deducted for sloppy rounding.
> 5. Always **draw the free body diagram** of each member with the assumed positive moment directions clearly marked — KTU examiners award step marks for clear diagrams.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Chord Rotation Formula** — $\psi = (\Delta_B - \Delta_A)/L$, with clockwise rotation being positive.
- **Modified Slope-Deflection Equation** — Includes the term $-3\psi$ inside the bracket: $M_{AB} = M^{F}_{AB} - (2EI/L)(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi)$.
- **Settlement FEM Magnitude** — $|M^{F}_{\text{settle}}| = 6EI\Delta/L^{2}$, sign determined by the direction of $\psi$.
- **Superposition Principle** — Total FEM = Load FEM + Settlement FEM (both computed separately, then added algebraically).
- **Sign Audit** — Most marks are lost in sign errors. Always redraw the deflected shape and verify the rotation direction.
- **Boundary Conditions** — Fixed end: $\theta = 0$; Pinned/roller end: $M = 0$; Settled support: $\theta$ is *unknown* but settlement $\Delta$ is *known*.
- **Joint Equilibrium** — The sum of moments from all members meeting at a joint must equal zero. This is the **primary equation** for solving unknown rotations.
- **Shear/Story Equilibrium** — For frames with side-sway, an additional equation $\sum M = 0$ for the whole frame is needed. (This is Module 3 territory.)
- **Unit Consistency** — Use $EI$ in $\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$, $L$ in $\text{m}$, and $\Delta$ in $\text{m}$ to get moments in $\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$.
- **Real-World Threshold** — Settlement above **$\mathbf{20\,\text{mm}}$** in framed structures warrants a re-analysis using this method.
- **Stiffness Invariance** — Settlement does **not** change the member stiffness coefficients ($4EI/L$, $2EI/L$); it only adds a *geometric* moment term.
