## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note to Students:** Per the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, Module 4 questions are framed as 14-mark problems with internal choice. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

### QUESTION A — Choice 1 [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion of an undamped SDOF system using D'Alembert's principle. The system has mass $m$, stiffness $k$, and is subjected to a harmonic force $P(t) = P_0 \sin(\omega t)$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported steel beam of length $L = 4$ m has a mass $m = 500$ kg placed at its mid-span. The flexural stiffness of the beam is $EI = 8 \times 10^6$ N·m². Compute:
- (i) The natural circular frequency $\omega_n$ and the natural frequency $f_n$.
- (ii) The natural period $T_n$.
- (iii) The maximum dynamic deflection if a harmonic force of amplitude $P_0 = 2000$ N acts at the mid-span at a frequency $\omega = 0.6\,\omega_n$. Assume undamped system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram & Assumptions:**
Assumptions: (i) Linear elastic behavior, (ii) Lumped mass model, (iii) Small displacements, (iv) Viscous damping neglected.

**Step 2 — Application of D'Alembert's Principle:**

$$\sum F = 0 \implies P_0\sin(\omega t) - ku - m\ddot{u} = 0$$

$$\boxed{m\ddot{u}(t) + ku(t) = P_0 \sin(\omega t)}$$

**Step 3 — Solution via Superposition:**

Homogeneous: $u_h = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$

Particular: $u_p = \dfrac{P_0}{k - m\omega^2}\sin(\omega t) = \dfrac{P_0/k}{1 - r^2}\sin(\omega t)$

Complete: $u(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t) + \dfrac{P_0/k}{1 - r^2}\sin(\omega t)$ **(3 marks for derivation)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $L = 4$ m, $m = 500$ kg, $EI = 8 \times 10^6$ N·m², $P_0 = 2000$ N, $r = 0.6$.

**Step (i) — Stiffness Calculation:**
For a simply supported beam with a concentrated load at mid-span:

$$k = \frac{48EI}{L^3} = \frac{48 \times 8 \times 10^6}{4^3} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^8}{64} = 6.0 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step (ii) — Natural Frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{6.0 \times 10^6}{500}} = \sqrt{12000} \approx 109.54 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{109.54}{2\pi} \approx 17.43 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step (iii) — Natural Period:**

$$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{109.54} \approx 0.0574 \text{ s}$$

**Step (iv) — Maximum Dynamic Deflection:**
The forcing frequency is $\omega = 0.6 \omega_n \implies r = 0.6$.

Static deflection:

$$u_{st} = \frac{P_0}{k} = \frac{2000}{6.0 \times 10^6} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

Dynamic Magnification Factor (undamped):

$$D = \frac{1}{1 - r^2} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.36} = \frac{1}{0.64} = 1.5625$$

Maximum deflection:

$$u_{max} = D \cdot u_{st} = 1.5625 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-4} = 5.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.521 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\omega_n \approx 109.54$ rad/s
- $f_n \approx 17.43$ Hz
- $T_n \approx 0.0574$ s
- $u_{max} \approx 0.521$ mm

---

### QUESTION B — Choice 2 [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** What is logarithmic decrement? Derive the expression for logarithmic decrement for an underdamped SDOF system. Show that for lightly damped systems, $\xi \approx \delta/(2\pi)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vibrating SDOF system exhibits successive peak amplitudes of $u_1 = 12$ mm and $u_6 = 4.5$ mm, where the subscript denotes 5 cycles apart. Determine:
- (i) The logarithmic decrement $\delta$.
- (ii) The damping ratio $\xi$.
- (iii) If the system has $m = 200$ kg and $\omega_n = 30$ rad/s, determine the damping coefficient $c$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:**
The logarithmic decrement $\delta$ is the natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes in the free-vibration response of an underdamped system.

**Derivation [5 marks]:**
The free-vibration response of an underdamped SDOF system is:

$$u(t) = U e^{-\xi\omega_n t} \cos(\omega_D t - \theta)$$

At successive peaks separated by the damped period $T_D = 2\pi/\omega_D$:

$$u_i = U e^{-\xi\omega_n t_i}$$

$$u_{i+1} = U e^{-\xi\omega_n (t_i + T_D)}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{u_i}{u_{i+1}} = e^{\xi\omega_n T_D} = e^{\xi\omega_n \cdot 2\pi/\omega_D}$$

Since $\omega_D = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \xi^2}$:

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{u_i}{u_{i+1}}\right) = \frac{2\pi\xi}{\sqrt{1-\xi^2}}$$

**Approximation for Light Damping [1 mark]:**
For $\xi \ll 1$, $\sqrt{1-\xi^2} \approx 1$, so:

$$\delta \approx 2\pi\xi \implies \xi \approx \frac{\delta}{2\pi}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $u_1 = 12$ mm, $u_6 = 4.5$ mm (i.e., 5 cycles apart, $n = 5$)

**Step (i) — Logarithmic Decrement:**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\frac{u_1}{u_6}\right) = \frac{1}{5}\ln\!\left(\frac{12}{4.5}\right) = \frac{1}{5}\ln(2.667) = \frac{1}{5}(0.981)$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 0.196}$$

**Step (ii) — Damping Ratio:**
Using the exact formula:

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\xi}{\sqrt{1-\xi^2}} \implies 0.196 = \frac{2\pi\xi}{\sqrt{1-\xi^2}}$$

Squaring: $0.0384 = \dfrac{4\pi^2\xi^2}{1-\xi^2}$

$$0.0384(1 - \xi^2) = 4\pi^2\xi^2 \approx 39.48\,\xi^2$$

$$0.0384 = 39.48\,\xi^2 + 0.0384\,\xi^2 = 39.52\,\xi^2$$

$$\xi^2 = 9.71 \times 10^{-4} \implies \xi \approx 0.0312 \quad (\approx 3.12\%)$$

*(Alternatively, using the approximation $\xi \approx \delta/(2\pi) = 0.196/6.283 \approx 0.0312$.)*

**Step (iii) — Damping Coefficient:**

$$c = 2m\omega_n\xi = 2 \times 200 \times 30 \times 0.0312 \approx 374.4 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\delta \approx 0.196$
- $\xi \approx 0.0312$ (3.12%)
- $c \approx 374.4$ N·s/m

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in D'Alembert's Principle:** Students often forget the negative sign in $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$. The inertia force always opposes acceleration.
> 2. **Confusing Damped vs. Undamped Natural Frequency:** Use $\omega_D = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi^2}$ for damped free vibration, but $\omega_n$ for undamped free vibration and forced vibration analysis.
> 3. **Resonance vs. Resonance-Like:** Resonance occurs at $r=1$ only for **undamped** systems. For damped systems, the peak occurs at $r = \sqrt{1-2\xi^2}$ (slightly less than 1). Examiners often test this distinction.
> 4. **Units Mismatch in Logarithmic Decrement Formula:** Always use $n$ correctly. If peaks are $n$ cycles apart, the formula is $\delta = (1/n)\ln(u_i/u_{i+n})$.
> 5. **Steady-State vs. Transient Confusion:** In forced vibration, the complete response has two parts. The transient decays; the steady-state persists. For long-term response, only the steady-state matters.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Equation of Motion (EOM):** $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = P(t)$ — the cornerstone of SDOF dynamics. Always derive using D'Alembert's principle.

- **D'Alembert's Principle:** Introduces fictitious inertia force $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$ to convert dynamics into statics. Always include it in your FBD.

- **Natural Frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$:** Depends only on mass and stiffness. Independent of amplitude or initial conditions (for linear systems).

- **Natural Period $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$:** Time for one complete oscillation in undamped free vibration.

- **Critical Damping $c_c = 2m\omega_n$:** Minimum damping required to prevent oscillation. Real systems have $c < c_c$.

- **Damping Ratio $\xi = c/c_c$:** Governs the regime:
  - $\xi < 1$: Underdamped (oscillates, decays)
  - $\xi = 1$: Critically damped (no oscillation, fastest return)
  - $\xi > 1$: Overdamped (no oscillation, slow return)

- **Damped Natural Frequency $\omega_D = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi^2}$:** The actual oscillation frequency in the underdamped regime.

- **Logarithmic Decrement $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\xi}{\sqrt{1-\xi^2}}$:** Measures amplitude decay. Used in $\xi \approx \delta/(2\pi)$ for lightly damped systems.

- **Harmonic Forced Response:** Steady-state amplitude $= D \cdot P_0/k$, where $D = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}$.

- **Resonance:** At $r = 1$, $D \approx 1/(2\xi)$ — peak amplification for lightly damped systems.

- **Phase Angle $\phi = \tan^{-1}[2\xi r / (1-r^2)]$:** The steady-state response lags the forcing by angle $\phi$.

- **Engineering Judgment:** Civil engineering structures typically have $\xi$ between 1% and 7%. Avoid operating frequencies near $\omega_n$ to prevent resonance.

- **Master Equation Trick:** Whenever you see a harmonic force $P_0 \sin(\omega t)$ or $P_0 \cos(\omega t)$, immediately define $r = \omega/\omega_n$ and write the DMF. This saves time in the exam.

- **Lab Connection:** Logarithmic decrement is the bridge between theoretical damping ratio and experimentally observed amplitude decay. It is the most commonly used method in vibration testing of structures.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transient and Steady State Responses, Simple Portal Frame Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST403 - ANALYSIS OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Transient and Steady State Responses, Simple Portal Frame Problems # 📘 MODULE 4 — TRANSIENT & STEADY-STATE RESPONSES IN STRUCTURAL DYNAMICS ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In **Structural Dynamics**, every dynamically loaded structure exhibits a time-dependent response $u(t)$ that is the **superposition** of two fundamentally different physical phenomena: 1. **Transient Response (Homogeneous / Natural Vibration Component):** The part of the total structural displacement that arises from the **initial conditions** of the system (initial displacement $u_0$ and initial velocity $\dot{u}_0$). It contains the structure's **natural modes of vibration** and decays with time due to **damping**. Mathematically, it is the solution of the *homogeneous* (unforced) equation of motion. $$u_{tr}(t) = \text{Complementary Function (C.F.) of the differential equation}$$ 2. **Steady-State Response (Particular / Forced Vibration Component):** The part of the total structural displacement produced **directly by the external time-varying forcing function** $F(t)$. It persists as long as the load is applied, oscillates at the **forcing frequency** $\Omega$ (not the natural frequency $\omega_n$), and does not depend on initial conditions. Mathematically, it is the solution of the *non-homogeneous* (forced) equation of motion. $$u_{ss}(t) = \text{Particular Integral (P.I.) of the differential equation}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (must memorize):** > **Total Dynamic Response = Transient Response + Steady-State Response** > $$u(t) = u_{tr}(t) + u_{ss}(t)$$ > The transient part vanishes after a few cycles of damped vibration, leaving only the steady-state oscillation. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Boating Lake" Imagine a small boat floating in still water (this is your **portal frame** in its equilibrium position). - You push the boat once and let go. It oscillates back and forth, gradually coming to rest. This self-decaying oscillation is governed **only by the boat's mass, stiffness, and water resistance** — *not* by your push. This is the **transient response** — born of initial conditions, dying out naturally. - Now, suppose a friend continuously pushes the boat forward and backward in a **perfect rhythm** (a sinusoidal force). After a few seconds, the boat settles into a stable to-and-fro pattern matching your friend's rhythm exactly. This persistent, rhythmic motion is the **steady-state response** — controlled by the forcing function, not by the initial push. - The total motion of the boat at any instant = small remnant of the initial wiggle (transient) + the rhythmic pattern (steady-state). > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold):** > - Natural circular frequency: $\omega_n$ — **rad/s** > - Damping ratio: $\xi$ — **dimensionless** (0 to 1) > - Damped natural frequency: $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi^2}$ — **rad/s** > - Forcing frequency: $\Omega$ — **rad/s** > - Frequency ratio: $\beta = \Omega / \omega_n$ — **dimensionless** > - Magnification factor (dynamic load factor): $D = u_{max}/u_{st}$ — **dimensionless** # 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Governing Equation of Motion (Single Degree of Freedom) For a linear elastic SDOF system representing a simplified portal frame (mass $m$, stiffness $k$, damping $c$) subjected to dynamic force $F(t)$: $$m\ddot{u}(t) + c\dot{u}(t) + ku(t) = F(t)$$ Dividing throughout by $m$: $$\ddot{u}(t) + 2\xi\omega_n\dot{u}(t) + \omega_n^2 u(t) = \frac{F(t)}{m}$$ Where: - $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — **undamped natural frequency** - $c_c = 2m\omega_n$ — **critical damping coefficient** - $\xi = c / c_c$ — **damping ratio** --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind the Solution **STEP 1 — Homogeneous (Transient) Solution:** Set the right-hand side = 0. The characteristic equation is: $$s^2 + 2\xi\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$ Roots (for underdamped case, $\xi < 1$): $$s_{1,2} = -\xi\omega_n \pm i\omega_d, \quad \text{where } \omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi^2}$$ Transient displacement: $$u_{tr}(t) = e^{-\xi\omega_n t}\left[A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right]$$ Apply initial conditions $u(0) = u_0$ and $\dot{u}(0) = v_0$: - $A = u_0$ - $B = (v_0 + \xi\omega_n u_0)/\omega_d$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The transient part is **bounded by an exponential envelope** $e^{-\xi\omega_n t}$ — it vanishes as $t \to \infty$. In **undamped** systems ($\xi=0$), the transient oscillation persists *forever*. **STEP 2 — Particular (Steady-State) Solution:** For a harmonic forcing $F(t) = F_0\sin\Omega t$: Assume $u_{ss}(t) = C\sin\Omega t + D\cos\Omega t$. Substituting into the equation of motion and matching coefficients of $\sin\Omega t$ and $\cos\Omega t$: $$C = \frac{F_0/m}{(\omega_n^2 - \Omega^2) + (2\xi\omega_n\Omega)^2/(\omega_n^2 - \Omega^2)}$$ $$D = \frac{-2\xi\omega_n\Omega \cdot (F_0/m)}{(\omega_n^2 - \Omega^2)^2 + (2\xi\omega_n\Omega)^2}$$ This is more elegantly expressed in amplitude–phase form: $$u_{ss}(t) = u_{st}\,\beta^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\xi\beta)^2}}\sin(\Omega t - \phi)$$ Where: - $u_{st} = F_0/k$ — **static deflection** - $\beta = \Omega/\omega_n$ — **frequency ratio** - $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\xi\beta}{1-\beta^2}\right)$ — **phase lag** --- ## 2.3 🧠 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Forced Vibration, SDOF) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | | 2 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi^2}$ | rad/s | | 3 | Critical damping | $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$ | N·s/m | | 4 | Damping ratio | $\xi = c/c_c$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Static deflection | $u_{st} = F_0/k$ | m | | 6 | Frequency ratio | $\beta = \Omega/\omega_n$ | dimensionless | | 7 | **Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF)** | $D = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\xi\beta)^2}}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Steady-state amplitude | $u_{ss,max} = u_{st}\cdot D$ | m | | 9 | Phase angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\xi\beta}{1-\beta^2}\right)$ | radians | | 10 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\xi}{\sqrt{1-\xi^2}}$ | dimensionless | | 11 | Total response (harmonic) | $u(t) = u_{tr}(t) + u_{ss}(t)$ | m | | 12 | Impulse response | $u(t) = (v_0/\omega_d)e^{-\xi\omega_n t}\sin\omega_d t$ | m (for $u_0=0$) | --- ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of This Framework - **Seismic Design:** Earthquakes are modelled as harmonic base excitations; engineers ensure $\beta \neq 1$ to avoid **resonance collapse**. - **Machine Foundations:** Rotating machinery exerts harmonic forces; portal-frame supports of gantry cranes are checked for steady-state amplitudes. - **Blast/Impact Loading:** Initial-velocity problems (e.g., vehicle impact on a frame) are pure transient problems. - **Bridge Dynamics:** Moving train on a bridge is a **transient** + **forced vibration** combined problem. # 📘 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Response to Harmonic Loading **Starting equation (undamped, $\xi=0$, SDOF):** $$m\ddot{u} + ku = F_0\sin\Omega t$$ **Divide by $m$:** $$\ddot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin\Omega t$$ **Assume particular integral of the form:** $$u_p(t) = A\sin\Omega t$$ **First derivative:** $$\dot{u}_p = A\Omega\cos\Omega t$$ **Second derivative:** $$\ddot{u}_p = -A\Omega^2\sin\Omega t$$ **Substitute into the equation:** $$-A\Omega^2\sin\Omega t + \omega_n^2 A\sin\Omega t = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin\Omega t$$ $$A(\omega_n^2 - \Omega^2)\sin\Omega t = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin\Omega t$$ **Match coefficients:** $$A(\omega_n^2 - \Omega^2) = \frac{F_0}{m}$$ $$A = \frac{F_0/m}{\omega_n^2 - \Omega^2} = \frac{F_0}{k - m\Omega^2} = \frac{u_{st}}{1 - \beta^2}$$ where $u_{st} = F_0/k$ and $\beta = \Omega/\omega_n$. **Transient (homogeneous) part:** $$u_{tr}(t) = B\cos\omega_n t + C\sin\omega_n t$$ **Total response:** $$u(t) = B\cos\omega_n t + C\sin\omega_n t + \frac{u_{st}}{1-\beta^2}\sin\Omega t$$ **Apply initial conditions** $u(0)=0$, $\dot{u}(0)=0$: - $u(0) = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0$ - $\dot{u}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C\omega_n + \frac{u_{st}\Omega}{1-\beta^2} = 0 \Rightarrow C = -\dfrac{u_{st}\beta}{1-\beta^2}$ **Final closed-form response (undamped, zero ICs, harmonic force):** $$\boxed{u(t) = \frac{u_{st}}{1-\beta^2}\left[\sin\Omega t - \beta\sin\omega_n t\right]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Observation:** When $\Omega \to \omega_n$ ($\beta \to 1$), the amplitude $\to \infty$ — this is the classical **resonance catastrophe** in undamped systems. KTU examiners love asking: *"What happens at $\beta = 1$ in an undamped system?"* --- ## 3.2 Derivation for Suddenly Applied Step Load $F_0$ (Constant Force) **Equation:** $$m\ddot{u} + ku = F_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = \frac{F_0}{m}$$ **Particular Integral (steady state):** $u_p = F_0/k = u_{st}$ (a constant) **Complementary Function (transient):** $u_c = A\cos\omega_n t + B\sin\omega_n t$ **Total response:** $$u(t) = u_{st} + A\cos\omega_n t + B\sin\omega_n t$$ **Apply $u(0)=0$, $\dot{u}(0)=0$:** - $0 = u_{st} + A \Rightarrow A = -u_{st}$ - $0 = B\omega_n \Rightarrow B = 0$ **Final result:** $$\boxed{u(t) = u_{st}\left[1 - \cos\omega_n t\right]}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Max dynamic deflection** = $2u_{st}$ (at $t = \pi/\omega_n$). > **Dynamic Load Factor (DLF) for step load = 2.0** — a classic KTU 2-mark question. --- ## 3.3 Application to a Simple Portal Frame (SDOF Idealization) **Problem Setup:** A single-bay, single-storey portal frame has: - Two columns of flexural rigidity $EI$ and length $h$ - Horizontal beam of infinite axial rigidity - Mass $m$ lumped at the beam level (from roof + supported machinery) - Subjected to horizontal harmonic force $F(t) = F_0\sin\Omega t$ at beam level **Equivalent Stiffness (for sidesway mode):** For a portal frame with fixed bases and rigid beam, the sidesway stiffness is: $$k = \frac{24\,EI}{h^3}$$ **Derivation rationale:** - Each column acts as a cantilever with moment release at the top → stiffness $3EI/h$ per column. - A portal frame's true stiffness is higher because the rigid beam restrains column rotation. - For **fixed-base portal with rigid beam**: standard result is $k = 24EI/h^3$. **Natural frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{24EI}{mh^3}}$$ **Steady-state horizontal displacement amplitude:** $$u_{ss,max} = \frac{F_0}{k}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\xi\beta)^2}}$$ **Bending moment at column base (steady state):** $$M_{base} = \frac{6EI}{h^2}\,u_{ss,max}$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Given:** A single-bay portal frame, $h = 4\,\text{m}$, $EI = 5\times10^6\,\text{N·m}^2$, lumped mass $m = 2000\,\text{kg}$ at the roof, horizontal harmonic force $F_0 = 10\,\text{kN}$ at frequency $\Omega = 10\,\text{rad/s}$. Damping ratio $\xi = 0.05$. Find steady-state amplitude and base moment. **Step 1 — Sidesway stiffness:** $$k = \frac{24 \times 5\times10^6}{4^3} = \frac{1.2\times10^8}{64} = 1.875\times10^6\,\text{N/m}$$ **Step 2 — Natural frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{1.875\times10^6}{2000}} = \sqrt{937.5} = 30.62\,\text{rad/s}$$ **Step 3 — Frequency ratio:** $$\beta = \frac{10}{30.62} = 0.3266$$ **Step 4 — Static deflection:** $$u_{st} = \frac{F_0}{k} = \frac{10000}{1.875\times10^6} = 5.333\times10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 5.333\,\text{mm}$$ **Step 5 — DMF:** $$D = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 0.1067)^2 + (2 \times 0.05 \times 0.3266)^2}}$$ $$D = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(0.8933)^2 + (0.03266)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.7980 + 0.001067}} = \frac{1}{0.8950} = 1.1174$$ **Step 6 — Steady-state amplitude:** $$u_{ss,max} = 5.333 \times 1.1174 = 5.959\,\text{mm}$$ **Step 7 — Base moment:** $$M_{base} = \frac{6 \times 5\times10^6}{4^2} \times 5.959\times10^{-3} = 1.875\times10^6 \times 5.959\times10^{-3} = 11{,}173\,\text{N·m} \approx 11.17\,\text{kN·m}$$ **Result:** Steady-state roof displacement = **5.96 mm**, Base moment = **11.17 kN·m**. # 📘 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Portal Frame SDOF Idealization (Block-Level Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Portal Frame
Real Structure] --> B[Lumped Mass m
at Roof Level] A --> C[Equivalent Spring
k = 24EI/h^3] A --> D[Equivalent Damper
c = 2m omega_n xi] B --> E[SDOF System
m u_ddot + c u_dot + k u = F of t] C --> E D --> E E --> F[Homogeneous Part
Transient u_tr t] E --> G[Particular Part
Steady-State u_ss t] F --> H[Total Response
u t = u_tr + u_ss] G --> H H --> I[Check Resonance
beta = 1 Avoid] ``` ## 4.2 Free-Body Schematic of a Portal Frame Under Harmonic Load ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FRAME[Portal Frame Geometry] C1[Column Left
EI, h] BEAM[Rigid Beam
Mass m lumped here] C2[Column Right
EI, h] BASE1[Fixed Base 1] BASE2[Fixed Base 2] end F[HARMONIC FORCE
F0 sin Omega t
Horizontal at Roof] DISP[Roof Sway
u t Horizontal] MOM[M_base
= 6EI u of t over h squared] BEAM --> C1 BEAM --> C2 C1 --> BASE1 C2 --> BASE2 F --> BEAM BEAM --> DISP DISP --> MOM ``` ## 4.3 Response Decomposition Tree (Transient vs Steady-State) ```mermaid flowchart TB TOTAL[Total Dynamic Response u t] --> TRANS[Transient Component] TOTAL --> STEAD[Steady-State Component] TRANS --> T1[Depends on Initial Conditions u0, v0] TRANS --> T2[Oscillates at Natural Frequency omega_n] TRANS --> T3[Decays with Envelope e power minus xi omega_n t] TRANS --> T4[Vanishes for Damped Systems as t grows] STEAD --> S1[Depends on Forcing Function F of t] STEAD --> S2[Oscillates at Forcing Frequency Omega] STEAD --> S3[Persists as Long as Load is Applied] STEAD --> S4[Independent of Initial Conditions] TRANS --> CRITICAL[CRITICAL CONCEPT: Always compute BOTH
and superpose] STEAD --> CRITICAL ``` ## 4.4 Dynamic Load Factor (DLF) Behaviour | Loading Type | DLF (max) | KTU Board Importance | |--------------|-----------|----------------------| | Suddenly applied constant load | 2.0 | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High | | Suddenly applied and removed (rectangular pulse) | up to 2.0 | ⭐⭐ High | | Linearly increasing ramp load | up to 2.0 (at $t = 2\pi/\omega_n$) | ⭐⭐ High | | Harmonic load (steady state) | $\dfrac{1}{\vert 1-\beta^2 \vert}$ (undamped) | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High | | Impulse load | 1.0 (sudden release) | ⭐⭐ High | # 📘 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 📝 PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Define transient and steady-state response of a structure subjected to dynamic loading. How are they mathematically represented?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The total dynamic displacement $u(t)$ of an SDOF system is the sum of two parts: $$u(t) = u_{tr}(t) + u_{ss}(t)$$ - **Transient response** $u_{tr}(t)$: the homogeneous (complementary) solution depending on initial conditions $u_0$, $\dot{u}_0$ and natural frequency $\omega_n$. It decays with time as $e^{-\xi\omega_n t}$. - **Steady-state response** $u_{ss}(t)$: the particular integral driven by the external forcing function $F(t)$. It oscillates at the forcing frequency $\Omega$ and persists as long as the load acts. For harmonic loading $F_0\sin\Omega t$, in the long-time limit the transient dies out and only the steady-state remains: $u(t) \to u_{ss}(t)$. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **A structure is subjected to a suddenly applied constant load $F_0$. What is its dynamic load factor (DLF)?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** For a suddenly applied (step) load on an undamped SDOF system with zero initial conditions, the response is: $$u(t) = u_{st}\left[1 - \cos\omega_n t\right]$$ The maximum dynamic displacement occurs at $t = \pi/\omega_n$ and equals $2u_{st}$. $$\therefore \text{DLF} = \frac{u_{max}}{u_{st}} = 2.0$$ Thus the structure carries **twice** the static deflection and **twice** the static internal forces under a sudden step load — a critical design consideration. --- ## 📝 PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Full Module Choice) ### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]` **(a)** A portal frame of height $h = 3\,\text{m}$ has two columns of $EI = 4\times10^6\,\text{N·m}^2$ with a rigid beam. A mass of $m = 1500\,\text{kg}$ is lumped at roof level. Compute the natural frequency of horizontal sway oscillation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A harmonic force $F(t) = 8\,\text{kN}\sin(15t)$ is applied horizontally at the roof. With damping ratio $\xi = 0.04$, compute the steady-state amplitude of roof displacement and the base moment in each column. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution **(a) Natural Frequency (7 marks):** For a fixed-base portal frame with rigid beam, sidesway stiffness: $$k = \frac{24\,EI}{h^3} = \frac{24 \times 4\times10^6}{3^3} = \frac{9.6\times10^7}{27} = 3.556\times10^6\,\text{N/m}$$ Natural circular frequency: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.556\times10^6}{1500}} = \sqrt{2370.4} = 48.69\,\text{rad/s}$$ Natural frequency in Hz: $$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{48.69}{2\pi} = 7.75\,\text{Hz}$$ Time period: $$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 0.129\,\text{s}$$ **(b) Steady-State Response (7 marks):** Given: $F_0 = 8\,\text{kN} = 8000\,\text{N}$, $\Omega = 15\,\text{rad/s}$, $\xi = 0.04$ Static deflection: $$u_{st} = \frac{F_0}{k} = \frac{8000}{3.556\times10^6} = 2.25\times10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 2.25\,\text{mm}$$ Frequency ratio: $$\beta = \frac{\Omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{15}{48.69} = 0.3081$$ DMF: $$D = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-0.0949)^2 + (2\times 0.04 \times 0.3081)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.8207 + 0.000608}} = \frac{1}{0.9067} = 1.103$$ Steady-state amplitude: $$u_{ss,max} = u_{st}\cdot D = 2.25 \times 1.103 = 2.482\,\text{mm}$$ Base moment (per column, with two columns sharing the load): $$M_{base,\,per\,column} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{6EI}{h^2} \cdot u_{ss,max}$$ $$M_{base} = \frac{3EI}{h^2}\cdot u_{ss,max} = \frac{3 \times 4\times10^6}{9} \times 2.482\times10^{-3}$$ $$M_{base} = 1.333\times10^6 \times 2.482\times10^{-3} = 3309\,\text{N·m} \approx 3.31\,\text{kN·m}$$ --- ### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **(a)** Derive the equation of motion for a damped SDOF system. Define damping ratio and critical damping. Explain the three cases: underdamped, critically damped, and overdamped. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steel portal frame of height 4 m, with column flexural rigidity $EI = 6\times 10^6\,\text{N·m}^2$, carries a suddenly applied horizontal load $F_0 = 5\,\text{kN}$ at the roof. Find the maximum horizontal displacement of the roof and the corresponding maximum base moment. Take $m = 2500\,\text{kg}$. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution **(a) Damped SDOF Derivation (7 marks):** Equation of motion: $$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = F(t)$$ Standard form: $$\ddot{u} + 2\xi\omega_n \dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = \frac{F(t)}{m}$$ Critical damping coefficient is defined as the smallest $c$ for which the system returns to equilibrium without oscillating: $$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$$ Damping ratio: $$\xi = \frac{c}{c_c}$$ Three cases from characteristic roots $s = -\xi\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\xi^2 - 1}$: | Case | Condition | Roots | Behaviour | |------|-----------|-------|-----------| | Underdamped | $\xi < 1$ | Complex conjugates | Decaying oscillation | | Critically damped | $\xi = 1$ | Real, repeated | Fastest non-oscillatory return | | Overdamped | $\xi > 1$ | Real, distinct | Slow non-oscillatory return | Most civil structures operate in the **underdamped** regime ($\xi \approx 0.02$ to $0.10$). **(b) Suddenly Applied Load Problem (7 marks):** Sidesway stiffness: $$k = \frac{24EI}{h^3} = \frac{24 \times 6\times10^6}{64} = 2.25\times10^6\,\text{N/m}$$ Static deflection: $$u_{st} = \frac{F_0}{k} = \frac{5000}{2.25\times10^6} = 2.222\times10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 2.222\,\text{mm}$$ For a suddenly applied constant load, the dynamic response is: $$u(t) = u_{st}\left[1 - \cos\omega_n t\right]$$ Maximum dynamic displacement (DLF = 2): $$u_{max} = 2u_{st} = 2 \times 2.222 = 4.444\,\text{mm}$$ Maximum base moment (per column): $$M_{base,max} = \frac{3EI}{h^2}\cdot u_{max} = \frac{3 \times 6\times10^6}{16} \times 4.444\times10^{-3}$$ $$M_{base,max} = 1.125\times10^6 \times 4.444\times10^{-3} = 5000\,\text{N·m} = 5.0\,\text{kN·m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Stiffness formula:** Using $k = 3EI/h$ (single cantilever) instead of $k = 24EI/h^3$ (portal frame) — yields a *very* wrong natural frequency. **(−2 marks)** > 2. **Confusing transient with steady-state:** Writing only the C.F. *or* only the P.I., not superposing both. Total marks = 0 if total response not given. **(−3 marks)** > 3. **Forgetting frequency ratio $\beta$:** Computing $u_{ss,max}$ without the DMF $D$ (i.e., just $u_{st}$). **(−2 marks)** > 4. **Sign error in phase angle** $\phi = \tan^{-1}(2\xi\beta/(1-\beta^2))$ for $\beta > 1$. The function is in the second quadrant. **(−1 mark)** > 5. **Units of $\omega_n$:** Writing rad/s as Hz or vice versa. **(−1 mark)** > 6. **Resonance answer:** Stating "$\beta = 1$ is safe" — WRONG. It is the most dangerous condition. **(−2 marks)** --- ## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST - ✅ **Total dynamic response** = **Transient (C.F.) + Steady-State (P.I.)** — the single most important identity. - ✅ **Equation of motion** in standard form: $\ddot{u} + 2\xi\omega_n\dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = F(t)/m$. - ✅ **Portal frame sidesway stiffness** for fixed-base, rigid beam: $k = 24EI/h^3$. - ✅ **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; period: $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$. - ✅ **DMF** for harmonic force: $D = 1\big/\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\xi\beta)^2}$. - ✅ **Resonance** (undamped): occurs at $\beta = 1$ ($\Omega = \omega_n$) — amplitude theoretically infinite. - ✅ **Step load DLF = 2.0** (undamped, zero ICs) — gives $u_{max} = 2u_{st}$. - ✅ **Base moment in portal column** (sidesway mode): $M = (3EI/h^2)\cdot u_{max}$ per column. - ✅ **Damping ratio** $\xi = c/(2m\omega_n)$; civil structures typically $\xi \in [0.02, 0.10]$. - ✅ **Phase lag** $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left[2\xi\beta/(1-\beta^2)\right]$ — always between 0 and $\pi$. - ✅ **Transient dies** as $t \to \infty$ for $\xi > 0$; **steady-state persists** for as long as the load acts. - ✅ **Critical damping** $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$ — defines the boundary between oscillatory and non-oscillatory decay. - ✅ **Frequency ratio $\beta$** classifies loading regime: $\beta \ll 1$ (quasi-static), $\beta = 1$ (resonance), $\beta \gg 1$ (vibration isolation). - ✅ **SDOF idealization of portal frames** is valid for single-storey rigid-jointed frames where mass concentrates at roof level.
